class_schedule.topic: Foundations of Organizational Behavior and the Manager's Role
learning_objectives: Define organizational behavior as a field and explain its three levels of analysis (individual, group, organizational)
Distinguish between management and leadership, and explain why both are necessary in modern organizations
Identify the core skills a leader must develop: technical, human, conceptual, and political
Evaluate the role of evidence-based management versus intuition and pop-management fads
readings: Robbins & Judge, Organizational Behavior (19th ed.), Ch. 1 - What Is Organizational Behavior?
Northouse, Leadership: Theory and Practice (9th ed.), Ch. 1 - Introduction
Kotter, 'What Leaders Really Do' (Harvard Business Review, December 2001)
Pfeffer & Sutton, 'Evidence-Based Management' (Harvard Business Review, January 2006)

lecture_notes.introduction: Every year, American companies spend more than $14 billion on leadership development programs, and yet Gallup's State of the Global Workplace report consistently finds that only 21 percent of employees are engaged at work. This mismatch is not a puzzle: most leadership training teaches techniques divorced from evidence, personality tests with no predictive validity, and platitudes that collapse the moment a leader faces a real decision. Organizational Behavior is the discipline that fixes this. It is the rigorous, evidence-based study of how people behave in organizations, drawing on psychology, sociology, social psychology, and anthropology to explain why some teams deliver and others implode, why some leaders inspire commitment and others generate only compliance, and why cultures that look identical on paper produce wildly different results. When Satya Nadella took over as CEO of Microsoft in 2014, he inherited a company whose culture Vanity Fair had called toxic and whose market capitalization had stagnated for a decade. Six years later, Microsoft had tripled in value, not because Nadella introduced a new product, but because he rewrote the company's cultural assumptions about learning, collaboration, and customer empathy. That transformation is the subject of this course. You will learn what we actually know about motivation, leadership, teams, and change, what evidence supports each claim, and how to translate that evidence into decisions you can defend. By the end of the semester, you will not just have read about leadership; you will have practiced it.
key_concepts: Organizational behavior (OB): the systematic study of individual, group, and organizational factors that affect behavior within organizations, with the goal of applying that knowledge to improve effectiveness
Three levels of OB analysis: individual (personality, perception, motivation, decision-making), group (teams, communication, power, conflict), and organizational (culture, structure, change)

Management versus leadership (Kotter): management is about coping with complexity through planning, budgeting, organizing, staffing, and controlling; leadership is about coping with change through setting direction, aligning people, and motivating

Robert Katz's skills model: technical skills (domain knowledge), human skills (working with people), and conceptual skills (seeing the big picture); the mix shifts as managers rise through the hierarchy

Effective managers versus successful managers (Luthans study): effective managers spend most time communicating and managing human resources; successful managers (fastest promoted) spend most time networking and doing politics; a cautionary finding about what gets rewarded

Intuition versus evidence-based management: leaders routinely rely on stories, experience, and consulting fads rather than replicated empirical evidence; Pfeffer and Sutton argue for treating management decisions with the same rigor as medical decisions
The replication crisis in OB: many classic findings (power posing, ego depletion, some priming effects) have failed to replicate, requiring skepticism even about textbook material
Contingency perspective: few OB findings are universal; most effects depend on context (industry, culture, task type, team composition), so a leader must diagnose before prescribing

Challenges facing contemporary managers: remote and hybrid work, generational differences, psychological safety, diversity and inclusion, ethical behavior under pressure, and the use of analytics in people decisions

Knowing-doing gap: most leaders already know what they should do (communicate more, give feedback, delegate, coach), but fail to do it consistently; the course emphasizes not just knowledge but deliberate practice
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case walk-through of Satya Nadella's first year as Microsoft CEO. Begin with the pre-2014 Microsoft culture: stack ranking, fierce internal competition, the know-it-all mentality, and a mobile business that had collapsed. Nadella's first all-hands memo in February 2014 introduced the phrase growth mindset (borrowed from Carol Dweck) and signaled a shift from know-it-all to learn-it-all. Discuss the specific decisions: killing the stack-ranking system, mandating empathy as a leadership criterion, requiring executives to read Marshall Rosenberg's Nonviolent Communication, the symbolic move of bringing iPhones on stage to demo Office for iOS, and the $26B LinkedIn acquisition as a cultural bet. Use the levels of analysis to decompose the turnaround: individual (hiring and firing choices), group (team behavior norms), organizational (culture change and strategy realignment). Close by asking students which decisions they would have made differently and why.
setup_requirements: Access to Harvard Business School case repository (student affiliate account)
Nadella, Hit Refresh (2017), excerpts from chapters 4 and 5
Gallup Q12 survey instrument for in-class engagement self-assessment
Slide deck with Microsoft stock price chart 2000-2024 and Glassdoor CEO approval ratings

review.value: Managers who rely on gut instinct and popular management books make predictably worse decisions than those who consult replicated empirical evidence; treating management like medicine (demanding evidence for interventions) is the foundation of this course

Kotter's distinction is not about hierarchy; it is about what you are coping with. Managing complexity requires planning and control; leading change requires vision and motivation. Most real jobs require both, but at different moments

Every OB problem can be decomposed into individual, group, and organizational factors. Diagnosing a sales team's poor performance requires looking at all three: wrong people (individual), dysfunctional dynamics (group), or misaligned incentives (organizational)

There is no single best leadership style, no best team structure, no best motivation approach. The right answer depends on context, and the manager's job is to diagnose context accurately before selecting a tool

class_schedule.topic: Personality, Individual Differences, and the Big Five
learning_objectives: Explain the Five-Factor Model of personality (OCEAN) and the evidence base that supports it
Evaluate the predictive validity of personality traits for job performance, leadership emergence, and counterproductive work behavior
Critique popular personality instruments (MBTI, DISC, Enneagram) against psychometric standards
Apply personality research to hiring, team composition, and self-awareness decisions
readings: Robbins & Judge, Organizational Behavior (19th ed.), Ch. 5 - Personality and Values
Barrick & Mount, 'The Big Five Personality Dimensions and Job Performance' (Personnel Psychology, 1991) - classic meta-analysis
Grant, 'Goodbye to MBTI, the Fad That Won't Die' (Psychology Today / LinkedIn, 2013)
Judge et al., 'Personality and Leadership: A Qualitative and Quantitative Review' (Journal of Applied Psychology, 2002)

lecture_notes.introduction: Every year, an estimated 2 million people take the Myers-Briggs Type Indicator as part of a hiring, coaching, or team-building exercise, and corporations spend roughly $20 million annually on MBTI licensing. Yet when Adam Grant reviewed the peer-reviewed literature, he concluded that the MBTI is little better than a horoscope at predicting job performance or leadership potential. Meanwhile, the Five-Factor Model of personality, developed over fifty years of empirical work, predicts performance, leadership emergence, counterproductive behavior, and even longevity with effect sizes strong enough to justify its use in hiring. The gap between what organizations use and what the science supports is enormous, and it exists because personality assessment is a market, and the most profitable instruments are not the most valid. This session separates the evidence from the marketing. You will learn what personality actually predicts, what it does not, how to interpret effect sizes honestly, and how to use personality information ethically. We will also confront a harder question: if conscientiousness predicts job performance across virtually every occupation studied, should organizations screen for it? What happens when valid prediction collides with fairness, stereotyping, and legal constraints? The answers are not obvious, and thinking them through is central to becoming a manager who uses evidence without weaponizing it.
key_concepts: Five-Factor Model (Big Five / OCEAN): Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Neuroticism; emerged from factor analyses of personality descriptors across languages and cultures

Conscientiousness is the most robust predictor of job performance across almost all occupations (meta-analytic r approximately 0.20-0.25), especially for task performance, attendance, and job retention
Extraversion predicts leadership emergence (being perceived as a leader) more strongly than leadership effectiveness (producing results), a distinction often blurred in popular writing
Neuroticism (emotional stability, reversed) predicts counterproductive work behavior, burnout, and turnover; highly neurotic employees struggle under stress but may perform well in predictable roles
Dark Triad: narcissism, Machiavellianism, and psychopathy; correlated with short-term leader emergence but long-term destructive outcomes, ethical lapses, and team damage

MBTI critique: forces dichotomous categories (E vs I) on continuous traits, has poor test-retest reliability (about 50 percent of test-takers get a different type on a second administration), and no predictive validity for job outcomes; still popular because it is nonthreatening and memorable
Core self-evaluations: a broader construct combining self-esteem, generalized self-efficacy, locus of control, and emotional stability; strongly predictive of job satisfaction and performance

Person-job fit and person-organization fit: the effect of personality on performance depends on whether traits match job demands (e.g., conscientiousness matters more in autonomous roles) and on cultural congruence

Legal and ethical constraints: US employment law under Title VII and the ADA restricts personality testing where it produces disparate impact or probes medical conditions; using unvalidated instruments creates legal and ethical risk

Self-awareness and feedback: even when personality tests are not used for hiring, the Big Five is a useful self-development tool because it forces honest reflection on traits that are difficult to change but possible to manage
lecture_notes.demonstration: In-class Big Five assessment using the public-domain IPIP-NEO-120 instrument (about 15 minutes). Students score themselves and receive percentile ranks on each trait. The instructor does not collect the results. Students then map their profile to the job they are currently pursuing: where is the fit strong, where is the gap? Next, work a case: a startup CEO is hiring her first head of sales. She receives two candidates: Candidate A scores 95th percentile on Extraversion and 30th percentile on Conscientiousness; Candidate B scores 60th percentile on Extraversion and 85th percentile on Conscientiousness. The meta-analytic evidence favors B (conscientiousness is the stronger predictor even in sales), but most managers pick A. Why? Discuss the availability heuristic, the confusion of leadership emergence with effectiveness, and how the CEO should weight the evidence against her intuitive reaction to the candidates in the interview room.
setup_requirements: IPIP-NEO-120 public-domain personality inventory printed or available online
Meta-analytic effect size reference table from Barrick & Mount (1991) and updates
Case packet with two anonymized candidate profiles for the sales-hire exercise
Projector for displaying effect size interpretation guide (Cohen's conventions)

review.value: Across almost every occupation ever studied, conscientiousness is the single best personality predictor of job performance. If you can only measure one trait, measure this one, and weight it seriously in hiring decisions

The MBTI has poor reliability and essentially zero validity for predicting job outcomes. It persists because it is comforting and nonthreatening, but using it for high-stakes decisions is indefensible
Extraverts get picked as leaders more often, but they do not necessarily perform better. Promoting the loudest person in the room is a predictable organizational failure mode

review.key: Personality Sets the Floor, Not the Ceiling
review.value: Traits are relatively stable but not destiny. A highly introverted person can be an effective leader by deliberately practicing the behaviors that leadership requires, even though it costs more energy than it does for an extravert

learning_objectives: Explain how perception shapes managerial judgments and why identical behaviors receive different interpretations
Apply attribution theory, including the fundamental attribution error and self-serving bias
Identify systematic decision-making biases: anchoring, availability, confirmation, overconfidence, and loss aversion
Design decision processes that counteract common cognitive errors
readings: Robbins & Judge, Organizational Behavior (19th ed.), Ch. 6 - Perception and Individual Decision Making
Kahneman, Thinking, Fast and Slow (2011), Ch. 1-4 and Ch. 17-19
Heath & Heath, Decisive (2013), Ch. 1-3
Tversky & Kahneman, 'Judgment under Uncertainty: Heuristics and Biases' (Science, 1974)

lecture_notes.introduction: In 2012, JC Penney CEO Ron Johnson, fresh from running Apple's retail stores, eliminated coupons and sales in favor of everyday low prices, killed the house brands that generated loyal traffic, and remodeled stores around small in-store boutiques. Within 18 months, the company had lost $985 million and Johnson was fired. Almost every step of the decision was driven by perceptual and attributional errors: Johnson attributed Apple's retail success to his own strategy rather than to Apple's product ecosystem (fundamental attribution error), he generalized from his experience at Target and Apple without testing in stores (availability bias), he refused to pilot the changes despite repeated recommendations from his team (overconfidence), and he dismissed internal dissent as resistance to change (confirmation bias). The JC Penney disaster is not unusual. Kahneman and Tversky's research demonstrated that executives, judges, physicians, and intelligence analysts make the same systematic errors, and those errors scale. A small perception bias in a hiring manager produces a biased team; a small decision bias in a CEO produces a failed company. This session is not about listing the biases so you can feel smart about naming them; it is about building decision processes that prevent them, because knowing about biases does not stop you from having them. Debiasing happens at the level of procedures, not individuals.
key_concepts: Perception as interpretation: people do not perceive reality directly but construct it through schemas, expectations, and attention; this is why two people witnessing the same meeting can give honestly different accounts
Selective perception: we notice information consistent with our expectations and filter out the rest; this is why performance reviews often say more about the reviewer than the reviewed

Attribution theory (Heider, Kelley): we explain behavior by attributing it to internal (disposition, character) or external (situation, context) causes; the attribution we choose drives how we respond

Fundamental attribution error: we over-attribute others' behavior to disposition and under-attribute to situation; when a colleague misses a deadline, we think lazy, when we miss one, we think I was overloaded
Self-serving bias: we attribute our successes to skill and our failures to circumstance; the asymmetric pattern distorts learning from experience and makes after-action reviews politically difficult
System 1 vs System 2 (Kahneman): fast, automatic, intuitive thinking versus slow, deliberate, effortful thinking; most managerial errors are System 1 masquerading as System 2
Anchoring: estimates are biased toward the first number seen; salary negotiations, budget projections, and sales forecasts are all vulnerable to anchors, even anchors that are obviously irrelevant

Availability heuristic: probability judgments are biased by how easily examples come to mind; recent dramatic events (a plane crash, a competitor's failure) dominate judgment even when they are statistically rare
Confirmation bias: we seek and remember evidence that confirms what we already believe; this is why pilot programs designed by the believers almost always succeed when they should fail

Overconfidence: calibration studies show executives routinely assign 90 percent confidence to predictions that come true 50 to 60 percent of the time; overconfidence is one of the most robust findings in behavioral research
lecture_notes.demonstration: Run the classic anchoring experiment live. Ask half the class to write down the last two digits of their student ID, then estimate the number of African countries that are members of the United Nations. Ask the other half to do the same without the student ID step. Tabulate the estimates. In nearly every classroom, the students who wrote a number first produce estimates correlated with that irrelevant number. Discuss the implications for budget meetings, salary negotiations, and performance ratings. Then work the JC Penney case in groups: for each major decision Johnson made, identify the bias at play, and design a specific decision process that would have caught the bias before it became a strategy. Close with the Heath brothers' WRAP framework: Widen your options, Reality-test your assumptions, Attain distance before deciding, Prepare to be wrong.
setup_requirements: Slips of paper and pens for the anchoring exercise
Projector with tabulation spreadsheet for live data collection
JC Penney case packet with Ron Johnson timeline and decisions
WRAP framework handout from Heath & Heath, Decisive

review.value: Research consistently shows that people who learn about biases still exhibit them. Debiasing must happen at the level of processes and decision structures, not at the level of individual awareness

When leaders consistently attribute poor performance to character rather than situation, they blame people instead of fixing systems, and they lose trust. The Toyota Production System is explicitly built around attributing defects to processes, not to workers
Overconfidence is pervasive. Executives who learn to distinguish their 60 percent confidence from their 95 percent confidence make dramatically better decisions, especially in uncertainty

Before committing to a major decision, imagine it has failed catastrophically and write the post-mortem. This activates the anti-confirmation-bias circuit and surfaces risks that the optimistic plan glosses over

class_schedule.topic: Motivation Theories: Needs, Equity, Expectancy, and Goal-Setting
learning_objectives: Compare content theories of motivation (Maslow, Herzberg, McClelland) with their empirical support
Apply process theories (Equity, Expectancy, Goal-Setting) to design effective motivational systems
Distinguish extrinsic from intrinsic motivation and explain when extrinsic rewards undermine intrinsic engagement
Diagnose motivation problems using the most evidence-supported frameworks
readings: Robbins & Judge, Organizational Behavior (19th ed.), Ch. 7 - Motivation Concepts
Locke & Latham, 'Building a Practically Useful Theory of Goal Setting and Task Motivation' (American Psychologist, 2002)
Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 'A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation' (Psychological Bulletin, 1999)
Pink, Drive (2009), Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: In 1968, Frederick Herzberg published 'One More Time: How Do You Motivate Employees?' in the Harvard Business Review. It became the single most-reprinted article in the magazine's history and shaped how generations of managers thought about motivation. The core claim, that satisfiers and dissatisfiers are independent dimensions, was elegant, memorable, and mostly wrong when tested with methodologies that did not have the same confound as Herzberg's original interviews. The history of motivation theory in organizational behavior is littered with beautiful ideas that did not replicate: Maslow's hierarchy is almost entirely unsupported by empirical testing, McGregor's Theory X/Y is a useful metaphor rather than a scientific theory, and Herzberg's two-factor theory is a methodological artifact. What does replicate? Goal-setting theory, expectancy theory, equity theory, and self-determination theory, all of which have hundreds of studies behind them and clear, specific implications for how managers should design compensation, feedback, and work itself. This session is a case study in how to read OB literature critically: the most famous theory is not always the most supported, and the most useful theories are often the least charismatic. When Google spent years studying what made its highest-performing teams tick in Project Aristotle, they did not find Maslow, Herzberg, or personality types at the top. They found psychological safety, dependability, structure and clarity, meaning, and impact, which line up remarkably well with the evidence-based theories covered in this session.
key_concepts: Content theories: describe what motivates people (needs, drives); include Maslow, Herzberg, McClelland; intuitive but weak empirical support

Maslow's hierarchy of needs (physiological, safety, belonging, esteem, self-actualization): proposes needs are activated in sequence; empirical tests do not support the strict hierarchy or the activation sequence, though the categories themselves are useful descriptively

Herzberg's two-factor theory: distinguishes hygiene factors (salary, working conditions, policies) that prevent dissatisfaction from motivators (achievement, recognition, work itself) that produce satisfaction; method-dependent finding that does not replicate with balanced methods

McClelland's needs: achievement (nAch), affiliation (nAff), power (nPow); the one content theory with reasonable empirical support, especially for predicting who seeks managerial advancement (high nPow, moderate nAff)
Process theories: describe how motivation works; include equity, expectancy, and goal-setting; stronger empirical support

Equity theory (Adams): people compare their input-outcome ratio to referents and experience tension when the ratios are unequal; explains why absolute pay matters less than relative pay and why secret compensation breeds distrust

Expectancy theory (Vroom): motivation = expectancy (effort to performance) * instrumentality (performance to reward) * valence (reward value); breaks when any of the three terms is near zero; used to diagnose why bonus plans fail

Goal-setting theory (Locke & Latham): specific and difficult goals produce higher performance than do your best or easy goals, provided the person is committed, receives feedback, and has the ability; one of the best-replicated findings in OB
SMART goals: Specific, Measurable, Attainable, Relevant, Time-bound; a popularization of Locke and Latham's work that is useful when applied with commitment and feedback

Self-determination theory (Deci & Ryan): intrinsic motivation requires autonomy, competence, and relatedness; extrinsic rewards can crowd out intrinsic motivation when they are contingent and controlling, especially for creative tasks
lecture_notes.demonstration: Workshop on diagnosing a motivation problem. Present the case of a struggling engineering team at a mid-sized software firm. Symptoms: declining code quality, missed deadlines, rising turnover. The director's first instinct is to add a quarterly bonus tied to story points delivered. Use expectancy theory to analyze: is effort linked to performance (the bonus is for points, but points depend on story estimation, which is gamed)? Is performance linked to reward (yes)? Is reward valued (for some yes, for others no)? Use equity theory: how will the bonus compare across senior and junior engineers? Use goal-setting theory: are the goals specific and committed? Use self-determination theory: will the extrinsic bonus undermine intrinsic motivation for the craftspeople who already care about code quality? Arrive at a more nuanced intervention that combines goal clarity, team-level recognition, autonomy in technical decisions, and a salary adjustment rather than a production-bonus system. Students present their diagnoses and the instructor highlights how the same symptoms yield different interventions depending on which theory is in use.
setup_requirements: Case packet: The Motivation Problem at Nimbus Software
Expectancy theory worksheet (E, I, V) for group exercise
Whiteboard space for diagramming diagnoses
Summary table comparing motivation theories by empirical support level

review.value: The hierarchy of needs is one of the most recognized concepts in management and one of the least supported by empirical research. Use it as a descriptive vocabulary, not as a predictive theory
Goal-setting theory is among the best-replicated findings in OB. Specific difficult goals beat vague or easy goals by a large margin, provided the person is committed and receiving feedback

For routine tasks, extrinsic rewards work fine. For creative or complex tasks where intrinsic motivation matters, controlling rewards can reduce engagement. Base pay fairly, then give autonomy rather than piling on contingent bonuses
Most motivation interventions fail because the manager diagnoses the wrong problem. Use expectancy theory to locate which link is broken (E, I, or V) before designing the intervention

class_schedule.topic: Motivation in Practice: Job Design, Rewards, and Engagement
learning_objectives: Apply the Job Characteristics Model (Hackman & Oldham) to redesign work for higher engagement
Evaluate the evidence on pay for performance, profit sharing, and gainsharing
Use the research on employee engagement to distinguish honest engagement programs from theater
Design recognition and non-financial reward systems that are equitable and motivating
readings: Robbins & Judge, Organizational Behavior (19th ed.), Ch. 8 - Motivation: From Concepts to Applications
Hackman & Oldham, 'Motivation through the Design of Work' (Organizational Behavior and Human Performance, 1976)
Lawler, 'Reward Practices and Performance Management System Effectiveness' (Organizational Dynamics, 2003)
Harter, Schmidt & Hayes, Gallup Q12 meta-analysis (Journal of Applied Psychology, 2002)

lecture_notes.introduction: Semco Partners, a Brazilian manufacturer, famously lets employees set their own salaries, pick their own bosses, and decide their own working hours. Ricardo Semler wrote two best-selling books about it, and it became a celebrated example of radical job design. Around the same time, Wells Fargo was running a different kind of job design experiment: an aggressive sales incentive system that pressured employees to open 2 million unauthorized customer accounts between 2011 and 2016, resulting in $3 billion in fines and the dismissal of thousands of employees. Both firms were applying motivation theory. The difference was in which evidence they used and how they thought about perverse incentives. Job design, compensation, and engagement are not separate topics; they form an integrated motivational system. Get any one of them wrong and the others cannot save you. This session covers what we actually know about designing work and rewards. You will learn how to redesign a boring job to increase intrinsic motivation using the Job Characteristics Model, how to think critically about pay for performance (which works well for simple tasks and poorly for complex ones), and how to separate real employee engagement from the vendor-driven engagement theater that now dominates corporate HR. The goal is not to maximize engagement scores on a survey; it is to build workplaces where people can do their best work because the work and the rewards make sense.
key_concepts: Job Characteristics Model (JCM): five core job dimensions (skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, feedback) combine through three psychological states (experienced meaningfulness, experienced responsibility, knowledge of results) to produce work outcomes

Motivating Potential Score (MPS): ((skill variety + task identity + task significance)/3) * autonomy * feedback; jobs scoring low on MPS can be redesigned by combining tasks, forming natural work units, establishing client relationships, vertical loading, and opening feedback channels
Job enlargement (adding horizontal tasks) versus job enrichment (adding vertical responsibilities): enrichment is more motivating because it increases autonomy and responsibility, not just variety

Pay for performance: works well for simple, measurable, individual tasks; becomes problematic for complex, interdependent, or hard-to-measure work because of gaming, narrow focus, and undermining of cooperation

Perverse incentives: reward systems often produce behavior that technically maximizes the metric but destroys the underlying goal (Wells Fargo account openings, Sears auto center repair fraud, Kobe Bryant and stat padding)
Profit sharing and gainsharing: organization-level incentives (ESOPs, Scanlon plans, Improshare) are less prone to gaming and better for collaborative work; effects are small but robust
Recognition as a reward: public, specific, timely, and non-monetary recognition is often more motivating than small bonuses, especially for younger employees; the key is specificity and credibility
Flexible benefits (cafeteria plans): let employees choose among benefits to fit their needs; evidence supports improved satisfaction and retention with minimal cost

Gallup Q12 and engagement research: twelve items predict business-unit performance across thousands of organizations; causation runs from engagement to performance, not just correlation, when measured longitudinally

Engagement theater: annual engagement surveys disconnected from action are worse than no survey, because they teach employees that feedback is ignored; real engagement programs close the loop with visible action within 60 days
lecture_notes.demonstration: Redesign exercise on a real job. Use the role of customer service representative in a health insurance call center, a job with notoriously low engagement and high turnover. Start by scoring the current job on the five JCM dimensions (typically very low on autonomy and feedback, moderate on identity and significance, low on variety). Compute the current MPS. Then apply each of Hackman and Oldham's implementing concepts: combine tasks (let one rep handle a claim end to end rather than routing it), form natural work units (assign reps to specific employer groups so they see outcomes), establish client relationships (direct phone number and accountability), vertical load (let reps make coverage decisions within limits), and open feedback channels (let reps see whether members used the care they were approved for). Recompute the MPS. Discuss the trade-offs: the redesigned job requires more training, pays more, and is harder to staff, but reduces turnover and improves member outcomes. This is the Mayo Clinic and USAA model of call center design. Close with a discussion of why most call centers do the opposite despite the evidence.
setup_requirements: JCM scoring worksheet with MPS formula
Sample job description for health insurance CSR
Before/after worksheet for the redesign exercise
Slide deck with USAA and Mayo Clinic call center case studies

review.value: Adding more tasks (enlargement) without adding responsibility does not increase motivation and often decreases it. Vertical loading (giving more control and accountability) is what makes jobs more motivating

review.key: Pay for Performance Is Dangerous for Complex Work
review.value: Individual incentives work for simple measurable tasks and fail spectacularly for complex interdependent work. The Wells Fargo scandal was a perverse incentive story, not a moral failing at the individual level
If you survey employees and do nothing, you are actively destroying trust. A good rule: do not ask unless you will act, and show the action publicly within 60 days
Specific, timely, credible recognition from a respected leader is one of the most cost-effective motivators available, and most organizations do it badly or not at all

learning_objectives: Distinguish groups from teams and describe Tuckman's stages of team development
Apply research on team effectiveness, including Project Aristotle's psychological safety findings
Diagnose team dysfunctions using Lencioni's five dysfunctions model and evaluate its empirical basis
Design teams for specific tasks by considering size, composition, and structure
readings: Robbins & Judge, Organizational Behavior (19th ed.), Ch. 10 - Understanding Work Teams
Edmondson, The Fearless Organization (2018), Ch. 1-3
Duhigg, 'What Google Learned from Its Quest to Build the Perfect Team' (NYT Magazine, February 2016)
Hackman, 'Why Teams Don't Work' (Harvard Business Review, 2009)

lecture_notes.introduction: Google's Project Aristotle set out in 2012 to answer a question that had no settled answer in the literature: what makes some teams dramatically more effective than others? Researchers studied 180 Google teams over two years, measuring every team composition variable they could think of: average IQ, extroversion, diversity, tenure, friendship outside work. Almost nothing predicted team performance. What they eventually found was psychological safety, a term coined by Harvard's Amy Edmondson in 1999 to describe a shared belief held by members of a team that the team is safe for interpersonal risk taking. Teams where members felt safe to speak up, admit mistakes, ask naive questions, and disagree with the boss outperformed teams that lacked that safety, and the effect dwarfed composition variables. That finding changes how we think about team management. The manager's primary job is not to assemble the right people (though that matters at the margin), it is to create the conditions under which any group of competent people can actually function as a team. This session grounds team effectiveness in the evidence: what Tuckman got right about stage development, what Lencioni got popular despite weak evidence, and what Edmondson and Hackman have taught us about the conditions that turn groups into teams. You will learn to diagnose a broken team in terms of inputs (composition, context), processes (safety, conflict, coordination), and outputs, and to design interventions that target the actual problem rather than whatever symptom is most visible.
key_concepts: Group vs team: a group shares information and makes individual decisions; a team has a collective work product that requires coordinated effort and shared accountability
Tuckman's stages: forming, storming, norming, performing, adjourning; useful as a descriptive framework, though teams do not always progress linearly and some never leave storming

Hackman's five conditions for team effectiveness: real team (stable, bounded, interdependent), compelling direction (clear purpose), enabling structure (right size, right norms, right tasks), supportive context (rewards, information, training), and expert coaching
Team size: Hackman argued five to eight is typically right; larger teams suffer coordination costs and social loafing; two-pizza rule at Amazon is a variant of the same insight

Psychological safety (Edmondson): shared belief that the team is safe for interpersonal risk-taking; distinct from trust (which is one-to-one) and from comfort (teams can be safe and high-conflict simultaneously)
Project Aristotle findings: psychological safety, dependability, structure and clarity, meaning, and impact; the composition variables almost all failed to predict team performance
Social loafing: in group settings, individuals often exert less effort than they would alone; increases with group size and decreases when individual contributions are identifiable
Groupthink (Janis): cohesive groups under pressure suppress dissent to preserve unity, producing bad decisions; the Bay of Pigs and Challenger disasters are classic cases

Lencioni's Five Dysfunctions: absence of trust, fear of conflict, lack of commitment, avoidance of accountability, inattention to results; popular but lightly evidenced framework, useful as a conversation starter

Team composition and diversity: functional diversity (different skills and perspectives) improves decision quality and innovation; demographic diversity has complex effects that depend on task and context
lecture_notes.demonstration: Run the Fearless Organization Scan as an in-class team health assessment. Split the class into teams of five or six who have already been working together in the course. Each team member anonymously rates statements like In this team, I can bring up problems and tough issues and People on this team sometimes reject others for being different on a seven-point scale. Aggregate by team and compare the profile to Edmondson's benchmark distribution. Teams below the median work through an intervention exercise: identify one specific behavior the team will start doing and one it will stop doing to raise psychological safety. Teams above the median work on a different exercise: identify one area where comfort has replaced challenge, and design a disagreement norm. Close with a discussion of how Edmondson's team at Harvard discovered the counterintuitive finding that higher-performing hospital nursing units reported MORE errors, not fewer, because safety made reporting possible. Link to the concept that suppressed problems do not go away; they just lose their audibility.
setup_requirements: Fearless Organization Scan instrument (Edmondson, 2018)
Anonymous response collection (Google Form or paper)
Team benchmark distribution handout
Discussion of the Edmondson hospital study and the Mulally Ford turnaround

review.value: Google's Project Aristotle found that who is on the team matters less than how the team works together. Psychological safety is the enabling condition, and it is within the leader's power to create or destroy

Teams should generally be five to eight people. Adding a ninth person does not add 12 percent more productivity; it adds coordination cost that often exceeds the contribution. Split large teams into smaller, focused teams with clear interfaces
Psychologically safe teams have more disagreement, not less, because members feel free to challenge ideas. Comfort-based teams produce politeness, silence, and bad decisions

When people do not feel safe reporting problems, the problems do not disappear; they grow quietly. Leaders who reward truth-telling (even inconvenient truth) reduce catastrophic failures and improve long-run performance

learning_objectives: Analyze communication barriers and the curse of knowledge in managerial communication
Apply trust research to build and repair workplace relationships
Deliver effective feedback using evidence-based models (SBI, radical candor, high-quality conversations)
Design communication systems for distributed and hybrid teams
readings: Robbins & Judge, Organizational Behavior (19th ed.), Ch. 11 - Communication
Scott, Radical Candor (2017), Ch. 1-4
Covey, The Speed of Trust (2006), Ch. 1-3
Edmondson, 'Learning from Failure' (Harvard Business Review, April 2011)

lecture_notes.introduction: Boeing's 737 MAX crashes in 2018 and 2019 killed 346 people and eventually cost the company more than $20 billion. The technical failure involved the MCAS flight-control system, but the deeper failure was communication: engineers who knew about the risk were not heard, the FAA was not fully informed, pilots were not trained, and a senior manager who raised concerns was moved out of the project. Every step was a communication breakdown, and every breakdown had a leader who preferred convenience or optimism over hard truth. Communication failures rarely look like communication problems at the time; they look like bad decisions, missed opportunities, or unexplained resistance. This session treats communication as a core leadership technology: not a soft skill, but the mechanism through which organizational knowledge is aggregated, errors are caught, and trust is built. You will learn why the curse of knowledge makes experts terrible communicators, why feedback feels so difficult and fails so often, and how trust, once lost, can be measurably repaired. We will also look at the specific challenges of distributed and hybrid work, where communication cues are impoverished and defaults are asynchronous. The manager who can communicate honestly, give feedback that actually lands, and build trust across distance has a competitive advantage that compounds every quarter.
key_concepts: Communication process: sender, encoding, channel, decoding, receiver, feedback, noise; each stage introduces potential for distortion, and communication is not a single transmission but an ongoing correction loop

The curse of knowledge: once you know something, it is nearly impossible to imagine not knowing it; experts explain poorly because they assume context the listener does not have (Chip and Dan Heath's tap-and-guess experiment)

Channel richness: face-to-face (highest, multiple cues and immediate feedback) > video > phone > email > memo (lowest, single channel, no feedback); rich channels for ambiguous messages, lean channels for simple information

Trust dimensions (Mayer, Davis, Schoorman): ability (competence in a domain), benevolence (good intentions toward the trustor), integrity (adherence to principles); trust is domain-specific, not global

Trust repair: violations of integrity are harder to repair than violations of ability; Covey's framework (commit, apologize, deliver, repeat) works for moderate violations; severe violations often cannot be repaired at all
Feedback models: Situation-Behavior-Impact (SBI) isolates a specific event and its effect; behavioral feedback is actionable, while trait feedback (you are disorganized) is defensive

Radical Candor (Kim Scott): care personally and challenge directly; the four quadrants (radical candor, ruinous empathy, obnoxious aggression, manipulative insincerity) help managers diagnose their own patterns
High-quality conversations: brief, specific, focused on behavior, collaborative rather than evaluative; research at Microsoft and Gallup shows short weekly conversations outperform annual reviews
Psychological safety and dissent: the best teams do not avoid conflict; they normalize it through explicit permission to disagree and structured dissent (red teams, devil's advocate roles)

Distributed and hybrid communication: defaults shift from synchronous to asynchronous, from rich to lean, from visible to invisible; leaders must deliberately compensate with video, written updates, and rituals of connection
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live feedback practice using the SBI model. Pair students and give each a scenario card describing a workplace problem: Your direct report interrupted a client on a call yesterday; Your peer took credit for your analysis in a meeting; Your manager canceled your one-on-one for the fourth time. Each student drafts an SBI statement: specific situation (when, where), specific behavior (what they did), specific impact (on you, on others, on outcomes). Pairs then role-play the delivery. Debrief by asking: who softened the feedback so much it became unclear? Who hardened it into attack? The goal is the middle: specific, clear, and grounded in care. Then introduce Kim Scott's quadrants and ask students to locate their own default pattern. Close with the two-column exercise from Chris Argyris: draw a line down a page, write what you said to your colleague on the right, write what you actually thought on the left. The gap is where communication fails and where leadership growth happens.
setup_requirements: SBI model handout with examples
Scenario cards for pair practice (one per student)
Kim Scott's Radical Candor quadrant diagram
Argyris two-column exercise template

review.value: Feedback lands when it describes a specific behavior and its specific impact. Trait feedback (you are unreliable) produces defensiveness; behavior feedback (you missed the Thursday deadline we agreed to, which delayed the team) produces change
You can trust someone to be competent in their area (ability) while distrusting their judgment in a different area. Treating trust as global leads to over-delegation in the wrong direction
Firing by email is not just cruel; it is ineffective, because lean channels cannot carry the emotional content. Match channel richness to message ambiguity

In-person teams communicate accidentally (hallway chats, body language). Distributed teams do not. Leaders must compensate with explicit rituals: weekly written updates, video defaults, documented decisions, and visible empathy

learning_objectives: Distinguish bases of power (French & Raven) and explain how different bases produce different compliance patterns
Analyze organizational politics and identify when politics are legitimate versus destructive
Apply Cialdini's principles of influence to leadership situations
Evaluate ethical boundaries of influence and recognize manipulation
readings: Robbins & Judge, Organizational Behavior (19th ed.), Ch. 13 - Power and Politics
Pfeffer, Power: Why Some People Have It and Others Don't (2010), Ch. 1-3
Cialdini, Influence: The Psychology of Persuasion (revised ed., 2021), Ch. 1-3
Grant, 'In the Company of Givers and Takers' (Harvard Business Review, April 2013)

lecture_notes.introduction: Power is the subject most treated as if it were dirty. Business schools teach strategy and finance without hesitation but ask students to role-play a power move and half the room gets uncomfortable. Jeffrey Pfeffer of Stanford has spent a career arguing that this discomfort is an expensive mistake. His observation: the people who think they are above politics lose to the people who engage with politics, and the losers often lose because they believed the myth that good work speaks for itself. Good work does not speak for itself. It needs an advocate, a coalition, and a leader who knows how to acquire and deploy power for purposes beyond their own advancement. The question is not whether to engage with power; it is how to engage with power ethically, effectively, and in service of something beyond personal gain. This session explores the evidence: where power comes from (French and Raven's bases), why different bases produce different compliance patterns, how Cialdini's principles of influence work in real situations, and where the line sits between influence and manipulation. We will also confront the harder question raised by Adam Grant: do givers or takers get ahead? The answer, surprisingly, is both ends of the distribution. The middle loses, and the givers who succeed have learned to protect themselves from being exploited. The goal is not to become a taker; it is to become an effective giver who knows how power works.
key_concepts: Power: the capacity of A to influence the behavior of B so that B does something B would not otherwise do; power is a relationship, not a property

French and Raven's bases of power: legitimate (formal position), reward (control of rewards), coercive (control of punishments), expert (specialized knowledge), referent (personal admiration and charisma)

Position power versus personal power: position power (legitimate, reward, coercive) comes with the role and leaves when you leave; personal power (expert, referent) travels with you and tends to produce commitment rather than compliance
Dependence as the foundation of power: A has power over B to the extent that B depends on A for something important, scarce, and non-substitutable (Emerson's dependence theory)

Organizational politics: the use of power to obtain outcomes not sanctioned by the organization; Pfeffer argues that what looks like playing politics often includes legitimate coalition building and advocacy
Cialdini's six principles of influence: reciprocity, commitment/consistency, social proof, liking, authority, scarcity; each is a heuristic shortcut humans use to decide quickly under uncertainty
Unity (seventh principle added in 2016): shared identity is a particularly strong influence lever, exploited positively in movements and negatively in radicalization

Upward influence tactics (Yukl): rational persuasion, inspirational appeals, consultation, ingratiation, exchange, personal appeals, coalition, legitimating, pressure; the effective tactics depend on the target and the request

Givers, takers, and matchers (Grant): givers cluster at both the top and bottom of performance distributions; the difference is whether the giver has the self-protective skills to avoid being exploited

Ethical influence versus manipulation: the line sits at consent and transparency. Informing someone's decision with true information is influence; exploiting a bias to produce a decision they would not make with full information is manipulation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Run the coalition-building simulation The Bargaining Game. Assign students to five departments competing for a limited budget, each with different formal power, expert knowledge, and alliances. The exercise runs 30 minutes of open negotiation and voting. Afterward, debrief with the French and Raven framework: which base of power did the winning coalition use? Which departments overestimated their legitimate power and failed to build coalitions? Which used expert power effectively? Discuss the hidden finding: in almost every run, the department with the most formal authority loses if it refuses to coalition-build, and the department with the least formal authority wins if it invests in relationships and expert credibility. This is Pfeffer's finding in miniature. Close with a structured reflection: where in your own professional life have you been avoiding politics because it felt beneath you, and what is the cost of that avoidance? Introduce the ethical boundary exercise: describe an influence attempt you have personally experienced that crossed the line from influence into manipulation, and identify specifically what made it cross.
setup_requirements: Bargaining Game simulation materials (role cards, budget rules, voting procedure)
French and Raven framework handout
Cialdini's seven principles quick-reference card
Grant's giver-taker self-assessment (short version)

review.key: Good Work Does Not Speak for Itself
review.value: Pfeffer's research is blunt: the belief that performance alone will be recognized and rewarded is the single most expensive career mistake. Leaders must learn to build coalitions and advocate for their work and their people

review.key: Personal Power Travels, Position Power Does Not
review.value: When you change roles, legitimate and reward power disappear but expertise and relationships remain. Invest in personal power because it is portable and because it produces commitment rather than mere compliance

Cialdini's principles work. The ethical question is whether you are informing a decision the person would endorse or exploiting a cognitive shortcut to get a decision they would disavow. The first is legitimate; the second is manipulation

review.key: Givers Can Win If They Protect Themselves
review.value: Grant's research shows that successful givers outperform takers in the long run, but unsuccessful givers get exploited. The difference is whether the giver maintains boundaries and distinguishes helpful from compliant

learning_objectives: Distinguish functional from dysfunctional conflict and identify conditions under which conflict improves performance
Apply the Thomas-Kilmann conflict styles framework to diagnose and adapt conflict approaches
Prepare and execute integrative (interest-based) negotiations using the Harvard Negotiation Project framework
Recognize common negotiation biases and counter them with deliberate preparation
readings: Robbins & Judge, Organizational Behavior (19th ed.), Ch. 14 - Conflict and Negotiation
Fisher, Ury & Patton, Getting to Yes (3rd ed., 2011), Ch. 1-5
Malhotra & Bazerman, Negotiation Genius (2007), Ch. 1-3
Jehn, 'A Multimethod Examination of the Benefits and Detriments of Intragroup Conflict' (Administrative Science Quarterly, 1995)

lecture_notes.introduction: In 2008, the United Auto Workers and General Motors negotiated an agreement that allowed GM to cut labor costs dramatically while preserving the union's existence. The agreement was painful for everyone, but without it GM would have liquidated rather than reorganized, and tens of thousands of additional jobs would have disappeared. The negotiation was a case study in integrative bargaining: both parties understood that failure meant shared loss, and both were willing to explore creative structures (the VEBA trust for retiree healthcare, tiered wages for new hires) that expanded the pie rather than fighting over its slices. Negotiation is the skill through which managers resolve most daily disputes, whether they call it that or not. When two product managers fight for engineering capacity, when a vendor pushes a price, when a direct report pushes back on a performance rating, when a partner proposes a joint venture, the underlying process is the same. Good negotiators prepare, separate interests from positions, generate options before committing, and use objective criteria to break deadlocks. Bad negotiators anchor on their first offer, confuse positions with interests, and treat every negotiation as a zero-sum fight. This session grounds conflict and negotiation in the best-replicated findings. You will learn when conflict is productive (often on tasks, rarely on relationships), how to diagnose your own conflict style and adapt it, and how to prepare a negotiation so thoroughly that the actual conversation becomes almost anticlimactic.
key_concepts: Functional vs dysfunctional conflict: functional conflict improves group performance by surfacing new ideas and preventing groupthink; dysfunctional conflict damages relationships and coordination

Types of conflict (Jehn): task conflict (substantive disagreements about work), relationship conflict (personal incompatibility), process conflict (how work gets done); task conflict can be functional up to a point, relationship conflict is almost always dysfunctional
Thomas-Kilmann conflict styles: competing (high assertiveness, low cooperativeness), avoiding, accommodating, compromising, collaborating; each style is right in some contexts and wrong in others
Distributive vs integrative negotiation: distributive splits a fixed pie (win-lose); integrative expands the pie through trades across issues of differing value (win-win)
Positions vs interests (Fisher & Ury): positions are stated demands; interests are the underlying needs and concerns; separating positions from interests is the foundation of integrative bargaining

BATNA (Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement): the outcome you will fall back on if the negotiation fails; your BATNA is the standard against which any proposed deal should be judged, and improving your BATNA is the most reliable way to strengthen your position
Reservation price (ZOPA): the walk-away point on each side; the zone of possible agreement is the overlap between the two reservation prices, and if there is no overlap, there is no deal available
Anchoring in negotiation: the first number strongly influences the final deal; the prepared negotiator either anchors first or has strong counter-anchors ready

Framing effects: the same deal described as a gain or a loss produces different responses; losses loom larger than equivalent gains, so framing concessions as gains to the other party is more persuasive

Negotiator's dilemma: the tension between claiming value (distributive) and creating value (integrative); pure claiming leaves value on the table, pure creating can be exploited by a distributive counterpart
lecture_notes.demonstration: Run the classic Sally Soprano integrative negotiation exercise. Sally is a semi-retired opera singer; Lyric Opera needs a last-minute replacement for a lead role. The students prepare in pairs, one as Sally's agent and one as Lyric Opera's producer. On the surface the conflict is over fee (Sally's last fee was much higher than the producer is willing to pay). In preparation, students must identify their BATNA, their reservation price, and their interests (beyond the fee: future career, artistic reputation, publicity, next season's contract). In negotiation, most pairs settle on the fee issue and miss the integrative potential. A few discover that a lower fee combined with a promise of a featured role next season and a public announcement of Sally's return serves both parties better than any single-issue deal. Debrief by tabulating the outcomes: how many deals were done, at what fee, with what additional terms? Discuss why the integrative deals emerged only in pairs who explicitly asked about interests beyond the stated position. Close with a structured preparation template that students will use for the major negotiation assignment.
setup_requirements: Sally Soprano case materials (Harvard Negotiation Project role cards)
BATNA / reservation price / interests worksheet
Thomas-Kilmann conflict style self-assessment
Negotiation preparation template (Fisher, Ury & Patton)

review.value: The question is not what you want them to agree to (your position); it is why you want it (your interests). Identifying interests on both sides is the door to integrative solutions

The strength of your negotiating position comes from the quality of your alternatives, not from your toughness in the room. The best time to improve your negotiating position is before you enter the negotiation, by building better alternatives

Task conflict at moderate levels improves decision quality by surfacing options and challenges; teams with too little conflict suffer from groupthink. Relationship conflict, on the other hand, is almost always destructive

The negotiator's dilemma is real. Pure value-claiming misses integrative opportunities; pure value-creating lets your counterpart walk away with more than their share. The skilled negotiator does both and watches for signals about which mode the other party is in

class_schedule.topic: Leadership Theories I: Trait, Behavioral, and Contingency Approaches
learning_objectives: Trace the evolution of leadership research from trait to behavioral to contingency approaches
Explain the Ohio State and Michigan studies on leader behavior
Apply the Hersey-Blanchard Situational Leadership model and evaluate its empirical support
Use Fiedler's contingency model and Path-Goal theory to diagnose leadership situations
readings: Northouse, Leadership: Theory and Practice (9th ed.), Ch. 2-4 and Ch. 5-6
Robbins & Judge, Organizational Behavior (19th ed.), Ch. 12 - Leadership
Stogdill, 'Personal Factors Associated with Leadership' (Journal of Psychology, 1948)
Fiedler, 'The Contingency Model and the Dynamics of the Leadership Process' (Advances in Experimental Social Psychology, 1978)

lecture_notes.introduction: For most of the 20th century, leadership research moved through a predictable arc. It started with the trait approach, which assumed great leaders were born, not made, and tried to find the personality traits that identified them. Dozens of studies produced inconsistent lists, and by the 1950s the trait approach was widely declared dead. It was replaced by the behavioral approach, which asked what leaders do rather than who they are, producing the Ohio State studies (initiating structure and consideration) and the Michigan studies (task-oriented and people-oriented leadership). These frameworks were useful but still could not explain why a leader who succeeded in one context failed in another. The contingency approaches (Fiedler, Hersey and Blanchard, Path-Goal) added the missing variable: the situation. The story of leadership research is the story of a field slowly realizing that context matters more than it wanted to believe. Today, we know that traits, behaviors, and situations all play roles, and that the best leaders diagnose their context and adapt their approach deliberately. Mary Barra's leadership at General Motors is a useful case study. Her promotion to CEO in 2014 came in the middle of the ignition switch crisis, which required very directive behavior and crisis management. Once the crisis stabilized, her style shifted toward a more participative, long-term approach focused on cultural change and the electric vehicle transition. Same leader, same company, dramatically different situation, different behaviors; that shift is what the contingency theories predict and reward.
key_concepts: Trait approach: identifies traits associated with effective leadership; modern meta-analyses show modest but consistent effects for intelligence, conscientiousness, extraversion, emotional stability, and openness
Great Man theory: the 19th-century predecessor of trait theory, based on studying historical figures; largely discredited because it ignored situation and context
Ohio State studies: identified two independent dimensions of leader behavior, initiating structure (task orientation, planning, role clarification) and consideration (supportiveness, trust, warmth)
Michigan studies: identified task-oriented and relationship-oriented leadership as two ends of a continuum (initially), later reconceptualized as two independent dimensions like Ohio State

The Managerial Grid (Blake & Mouton): combines concern for production and concern for people into a 9x9 grid; popular for training, though empirical support for the 9,9 team leader as universally best is mixed

Fiedler's contingency model: leadership effectiveness depends on the match between leader style (LPC score, measured by rating the least preferred coworker) and situational favorableness (leader-member relations, task structure, position power)

Implications of Fiedler's model: task-motivated leaders perform best in very favorable or very unfavorable situations; relationship-motivated leaders perform best in moderately favorable situations; leaders should be matched to situations rather than trained to change

Hersey-Blanchard Situational Leadership: leader behavior should adapt to follower readiness (combination of ability and willingness); four styles (telling, selling, participating, delegating) map to four readiness levels

Empirical support for Situational Leadership: the model is enormously popular in training, but rigorous empirical tests provide inconsistent support; the core insight (adjust to follower readiness) is valid even if the specific prescriptions are not uniformly verified

Path-Goal theory (House): the leader's job is to clear obstacles and provide rewards along the follower's path to goals; leader behavior (directive, supportive, participative, achievement-oriented) should be selected based on follower characteristics and environmental factors
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case comparison exercise using Hersey-Blanchard Situational Leadership. Present four employees at the same company who all have been asked to lead the same new initiative: (1) a veteran engineer, confident, experienced, and eager; (2) a new hire, smart but still learning the domain, enthusiastic but uncertain; (3) a mid-career engineer who has become disengaged over the past year, technically skilled but unwilling; (4) a senior manager with strong people skills but no domain expertise, willing but temporarily uncertain about the content. Students identify the readiness level for each and select an appropriate style (delegating, telling, selling, or participating). Then complicate the case: the new hire's uncertainty turns out to be a deeper confidence issue that telling alone will not address; the disengaged engineer needs a conversation about what changed, not just directive oversight. Use the cases to show both the usefulness and the limits of the framework: it is a diagnostic aid, not a prescription generator. Close with a discussion of Mary Barra's GM turnaround and how her style shifted as the situation evolved from crisis to strategic transformation.
setup_requirements: Hersey-Blanchard Situational Leadership II diagram and handout
Four employee case descriptions for the diagnosis exercise
Fiedler's LPC contingency model matrix
Mary Barra timeline (2014 ignition switch crisis through 2024 EV transition)

review.value: Decades of leadership research converge on one central finding: the best style depends on the situation. Leaders who adapt outperform leaders who are consistent in the wrong direction

Hersey-Blanchard's key insight is that the same direction applied to followers with different readiness levels produces different results. Matching directive or supportive behavior to follower capability is one of the most underused leadership skills

The trait approach is not dead. Meta-analyses show real, replicable effects for intelligence and conscientiousness on leadership effectiveness. But the effects are modest, and situation and behavior matter more

Unlike traits, leader behaviors can be learned. Initiating structure and consideration are skills that can be deliberately practiced, and that practice produces measurable improvement in team outcomes

class_schedule.topic: Leadership Theories II: Transformational, Servant, and Authentic Leadership
learning_objectives: Distinguish transactional from transformational leadership and explain Bass's augmentation hypothesis
Describe the four components of transformational leadership (the four I's)
Compare servant leadership (Greenleaf) and authentic leadership frameworks
Evaluate the empirical evidence for modern leadership theories and identify their limits
readings: Northouse, Leadership: Theory and Practice (9th ed.), Ch. 8-10 (Transformational, Authentic, Servant)
Bass, 'From Transactional to Transformational Leadership: Learning to Share the Vision' (Organizational Dynamics, 1990)
George, Authentic Leadership (2003), Ch. 1-2
Greenleaf, 'The Servant as Leader' (1970)

lecture_notes.introduction: Indra Nooyi, CEO of PepsiCo from 2006 to 2018, is a textbook example of transformational leadership. She set a bold vision called Performance with Purpose that reframed PepsiCo as a company responsible for long-term health outcomes, environmental sustainability, and employee wellbeing, not just quarterly returns. She made costly decisions (reducing salt and sugar across the product portfolio, investing in healthier brands like Quaker and Tropicana) that Wall Street initially punished, and she spent years explaining and re-explaining the strategy until the market caught up. During her tenure, PepsiCo's revenue grew 80 percent and its commitment to the purpose strategy survived both analyst skepticism and board pressure. Contrast this with an earlier era of PepsiCo leaders who focused on quarterly targets and market share. Both approaches work in some sense, but they produce very different organizations. This session explores the theories that have dominated leadership research since the 1980s: transformational leadership (Bass), servant leadership (Greenleaf), and authentic leadership (George). All three emphasize the relational, values-based, and long-term dimensions of leadership that earlier theories underplayed. All three have generated substantial empirical support, especially for transformational leadership, which has been studied in dozens of countries and hundreds of contexts. But they also have limits and critics. The session closes with a hard question: are we moving from evidence-based leadership theories toward inspirational narratives dressed up as science? The answer is nuanced, and understanding the line between them is part of becoming a critical consumer of leadership research.
key_concepts: Transactional leadership: leader-follower exchanges of rewards for performance; effective at baseline but does not inspire extraordinary effort
Transformational leadership (Burns, Bass): leader raises followers' motivation and moral maturity by appealing to higher values and a shared vision; produces commitment rather than compliance
Bass's augmentation hypothesis: transformational leadership augments transactional, producing effort and performance beyond what transactional alone can achieve

The four I's of transformational leadership: Idealized Influence (role modeling), Inspirational Motivation (vision and communication), Intellectual Stimulation (challenging assumptions), Individualized Consideration (coaching and attending to each follower)
MLQ (Multifactor Leadership Questionnaire): the most widely used instrument for measuring transformational and transactional leadership; extensive validation but some factor structure controversies

Servant leadership (Greenleaf): the leader's primary role is to serve followers' growth and wellbeing; the test of servant leadership is whether followers become healthier, wiser, freer, and more likely to themselves become servants

Servant leadership components (Liden et al. 2008): emotional healing, creating value for the community, conceptual skills, empowering, helping subordinates grow, putting subordinates first, behaving ethically
Authentic leadership (George, Avolio, Walumbwa): self-awareness, relational transparency, balanced processing, and internalized moral perspective; emerged in response to post-Enron ethical crises

Criticisms of contemporary leadership theories: risk of romanticizing leaders, conflation of description with prescription, measurement issues (self-report leaders rating themselves), and cultural specificity (most research is Western)

Dark side of transformational leadership: charismatic leaders can manipulate, and the line between inspiration and cult-like devotion is thinner than most research acknowledges (Jim Jones, Elizabeth Holmes, Adam Neumann)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Leadership profile comparison workshop. Assign each small group a well-known CEO to study: Satya Nadella (Microsoft), Indra Nooyi (PepsiCo), Mary Barra (GM), Howard Schultz (Starbucks), Ken Frazier (Merck). Provide each group with a packet containing the CEO's communications (letters to shareholders, key speeches, memoir excerpts) and MLQ-style behavioral exemplars. Groups identify evidence of each of the four I's and map the leader's style against transformational, servant, and authentic frameworks. Each group presents a five-minute leadership profile. Debrief by asking: which framework best describes each leader, and what do the mismatches tell us about the limits of the frameworks? Close with the dark side exercise: apply the same transformational leadership framework to Elizabeth Holmes at Theranos. She displayed many of the same behaviors (inspirational motivation, idealized influence, vision), but the substance was fraudulent. What separates healthy from pathological transformational leadership? The answer points to authenticity (internal values), integrity (truth in vision), and servant orientation (followers' growth) as the guardrails that keep transformational leadership from becoming manipulation.
setup_requirements: CEO communication packets for each study CEO
MLQ-style behavioral exemplar reference sheet
Four I's framework diagram
Elizabeth Holmes / Theranos timeline as counter-example

review.value: Transformational leadership does not replace transactional; it adds to it. Leaders still need to set clear expectations and deliver rewards, but they can achieve extraordinary effort on top of that by inspiring vision and individual development

Idealized influence, inspirational motivation, intellectual stimulation, and individualized consideration are behaviors, not traits. They can be developed through deliberate practice, 360-degree feedback, and coaching

Charismatic leaders can inspire followers in the wrong direction. The distinguishing feature of healthy transformational leadership is authenticity: alignment between stated values and actual behavior, transparency about uncertainty, and willingness to hear dissenting views

Greenleaf's test is whether followers are healthier, wiser, freer, and more autonomous as a result of your leadership. This reframes success from the leader's achievements to the team's growth, and it changes what daily decisions look like

learning_objectives: Define organizational culture and distinguish it from climate
Analyze culture using Schein's three levels (artifacts, espoused values, underlying assumptions)
Apply the Competing Values Framework and the OCAI to diagnose culture
Explain how culture is transmitted, reinforced, and changed
readings: Robbins & Judge, Organizational Behavior (19th ed.), Ch. 16 - Organizational Culture
Schein, Organizational Culture and Leadership (5th ed., 2017), Ch. 1-4
Cameron & Quinn, Diagnosing and Changing Organizational Culture (3rd ed., 2011), Ch. 1-3
Groysberg, Lee, Price & Cheng, 'The Leader's Guide to Corporate Culture' (Harvard Business Review, January 2018)

lecture_notes.introduction: In 2015, Travis Kalanick's Uber was one of the fastest-growing startups in history. By 2017, a blog post by former engineer Susan Fowler describing sexual harassment and a broken HR process triggered a cascade of scandals, investigations, executive departures, and Kalanick's resignation. The technical product still worked, the business model still worked, the growth still worked, but the culture had become toxic enough that external events could no longer be contained. Culture is difficult to define and easier to feel. Peter Drucker is widely credited with saying culture eats strategy for breakfast, and while the attribution is disputed, the insight holds. Strategy can be copied; culture cannot, and culture determines how strategy actually gets executed. This session grounds culture in the strongest available frameworks. Edgar Schein's three-level model is the dominant academic framework, distinguishing what you see (artifacts) from what people say (espoused values) from what actually drives behavior (underlying assumptions). The Competing Values Framework gives you a measurable way to diagnose culture and compare it to industry norms. The Harvard Business Review's 2018 leadership guide synthesizes decades of research into a practical typology. You will learn how culture is transmitted (stories, rituals, symbols, language), how it is reinforced (hiring, socialization, rewards), and how it is changed (a slow, deliberate process that cannot be accomplished by posters or town halls alone). The Microsoft culture shift under Satya Nadella is the most instructive recent case because it is well-documented, measurable, and deliberate.
key_concepts: Culture definition (Schein): a pattern of shared basic assumptions learned by a group as it solved its problems of external adaptation and internal integration, which has worked well enough to be considered valid and therefore taught to new members as the correct way to perceive, think, and feel

Three levels of culture (Schein): artifacts (visible products and behaviors), espoused values (stated strategies, goals, philosophies), underlying assumptions (unconscious beliefs and taken-for-granted views)
Climate vs culture: climate is the perceived, measurable surface (engagement, communication quality, fairness); culture is the deeper structure that produces those perceptions
Dominant vs subcultures: large organizations have both a dominant culture and subcultures (by function, region, business unit) that may align or conflict with the dominant culture
Strong vs weak cultures: strong cultures have widely shared and intensely held values; strong culture is an advantage when the values are right and a liability when the environment shifts

Competing Values Framework: two dimensions (internal focus vs external focus; flexibility vs stability) produce four culture types: clan (collaborate), adhocracy (create), market (compete), hierarchy (control)

OCAI (Organizational Culture Assessment Instrument): measures both current and preferred culture on the Competing Values dimensions; useful for diagnosing gaps between where the culture is and where leaders want it to go
Culture transmission: stories, rituals, material symbols, language; these are the everyday mechanisms through which culture is learned by new members

Socialization stages: pre-arrival (expectations), encounter (reality shock), metamorphosis (internalization); strong socialization programs shape new hires into the culture, though they also reduce diversity of thought

Culture change: one of the hardest management tasks; requires clear new vision, leadership modeling, visible early wins, structural reinforcement (hiring, promotions, rewards), and sustained attention over years, not quarters
lecture_notes.demonstration: Culture diagnosis exercise. Each group receives a packet with artifacts from a real company: job postings, press releases, employee review excerpts, office photos, leadership quotes. Groups use Schein's three levels to analyze: what do the artifacts reveal? What espoused values does the company state publicly? What underlying assumptions can you infer from actual behavior (who gets promoted, what gets rewarded, what gets punished)? Often the three levels conflict, and that conflict is diagnostic. A company with collaborative espoused values but individual ranking for bonuses has a culture that rewards competition beneath a collaborative veneer. Then apply the Competing Values Framework: which of the four cultures dominates? Compare the current culture to what the espoused strategy requires. Identify the gap and discuss what changing the culture would require. Close with the Microsoft case: Satya Nadella's cultural transformation from know-it-all to learn-it-all was not accomplished through a poster campaign. It required new hiring criteria, new promotion criteria, new language in leadership communications, a rewrite of the performance review system, public modeling from the top, and years of sustained attention. Students identify the parallel moves that would be required in the company they analyzed.
setup_requirements: Company artifact packets (three to four real companies)
Schein's three-level model template
OCAI current/preferred scoring sheet
Microsoft culture change timeline (2014-2020)
