review.value: You cannot diagnose culture from its visible artifacts alone. The open-plan office, the ping-pong table, and the values poster may have no relationship to the underlying assumptions that actually drive behavior

The fastest way to see what a culture actually values is to look at who gets promoted and who gets fired. If the espoused values and the promotion patterns contradict each other, the promotion patterns are the real culture

review.key: Culture Change Is Slow and Structural
review.value: Culture does not change because leaders announce a new vision. It changes when hiring, promotions, rewards, and daily leadership behavior change, and when the changes are sustained for years

A strong culture is a competitive advantage when the environment rewards the culture's values and a liability when the environment shifts. Adaptability requires some willingness to question even deeply held assumptions

learning_objectives: Distinguish planned change from emergent change and describe sources of change
Apply Lewin's three-step change model (unfreeze, change, refreeze) to a specific change initiative
Apply Kotter's 8-step model and explain why 70 percent of change efforts fail
Diagnose and address resistance to change using evidence-based tactics
readings: Robbins & Judge, Organizational Behavior (19th ed.), Ch. 17 - Organizational Change and Stress Management
Kotter, Leading Change (2012 ed.), Ch. 1-5
Kotter, 'Leading Change: Why Transformation Efforts Fail' (Harvard Business Review, January 2007)
Heath & Heath, Switch (2010), Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: John Kotter's 1995 Harvard Business Review article 'Leading Change: Why Transformation Efforts Fail' opened with a number that shocked the management world: 70 percent of organizational change efforts fail to achieve their goals. That figure has been repeated endlessly and is sometimes challenged, but the underlying observation is robust: change is hard, most attempts fall short, and the failures follow predictable patterns. Kotter's contribution was to turn the failure patterns into a prescription. His 8-step model is almost impossible to avoid in any business discussion of change management, and for good reason: it captures most of what research has found to matter, in an order that reflects how successful change actually unfolds. This session covers change management not as a slide deck but as a practice. You will learn why people resist change (often for rational reasons), why urgency has to come before planning and not after, why coalitions matter more than individual leaders, and why anchoring new behaviors in the culture is what separates change that sticks from change that reverts. We will look at two contrasting cases: the Ford turnaround under Alan Mulally, which followed almost every Kotter step deliberately and succeeded, and the Hewlett-Packard merger with Compaq under Carly Fiorina, which attempted change without the foundation steps and generated years of organizational damage. The goal is to equip you to lead change initiatives that have a realistic chance of success rather than adding to the 70 percent failure statistic.
key_concepts: Forces for change: technology, economic shocks, competition, social trends, world politics, demographics; change is continuous, and organizations that do not adapt do not survive

Lewin's three-step model: unfreeze (create motivation to change), change (move to new state), refreeze (stabilize and institutionalize); simple but powerful framework that underlies most change models

Kotter's 8-step model: (1) establish urgency, (2) form a powerful guiding coalition, (3) create a vision, (4) communicate the vision, (5) empower broad-based action, (6) generate short-term wins, (7) consolidate gains and produce more change, (8) anchor new approaches in the culture
Why urgency matters: without a sense of urgency, people will not sacrifice comfort for change; Kotter argues this is the most common failure point and the one that dooms initiatives before they begin

Guiding coalition: change requires a team with credibility, expertise, position power, and leadership, not a single hero; the coalition provides the combination of skills that individual leaders cannot

Communication as repetition: the vision must be communicated repeatedly (Kotter suggests a factor of ten more than leaders think is necessary) through multiple channels, with consistent messaging and visible modeling

Short-term wins: visible improvements within 6 to 18 months are essential for maintaining momentum, converting skeptics, and funding the longer effort; wins must be unambiguous and attributable to the change

Resistance to change: rational (legitimate concerns about unknowns and losses), political (power shifts), emotional (attachment to the old way), and cultural (identity threats); each source requires a different response

Kurt Lewin's Force Field Analysis: mapping driving forces and restraining forces; change comes from increasing drivers or reducing restrainers, and reducing restrainers is often more effective because it produces less tension

Anchoring in culture: the final step is the hardest and the most often skipped; until new behaviors become the way we do things here, the change is fragile and can revert the moment leaders shift attention
lecture_notes.demonstration: Change case exercise using the Ford Motor Company turnaround under Alan Mulally (2006-2014). Mulally took over a company that was losing $17 billion a year, had a famously siloed and internally competitive culture, and was about to require government bailouts to survive. Walk through each of Kotter's 8 steps using Mulally's actual decisions: urgency (showing everyone the balance sheet), coalition (bringing in outside and inside leaders), vision (One Ford with a global product strategy), communication (the weekly Business Plan Review meeting where all top executives reported color-coded status), empowerment (forcing executives to admit problems rather than hide them, famously applauding Mark Fields's first red status), short-term wins (profitability within 18 months), consolidation (restructured debt, divested Volvo and Jaguar), anchoring (the BPR meeting itself became cultural infrastructure). Contrast with the Carly Fiorina HP-Compaq merger, which tried to move through change without establishing urgency, without a credible coalition, without early wins, and with a communication approach that generated resistance rather than commitment. Students identify the specific steps that went missing and discuss what Fiorina could have done differently. Close with a structured change plan template that students will use in the final project.
setup_requirements: Ford turnaround case packet and BPR meeting description
Kotter 8-step framework handout with prompts
HP-Compaq merger timeline and outcomes
Force Field Analysis template for student exercise

review.value: Skipping the urgency step is the most common reason change fails. People do not abandon comfort without a clear reason, and that reason must be specific, visible, and emotionally resonant
Successful change requires a team with the right mix of credibility, expertise, and position power. Solo leaders, no matter how charismatic, cannot carry a transformation through all 8 steps

Resistance to change is often diagnosed as stubbornness or emotional attachment. In most cases, resisters have legitimate concerns that the leader has not addressed. Treating resistance as information rather than obstacle is more productive

Change that is not anchored in hiring, promotions, rewards, and daily leadership behavior reverts as soon as attention shifts. Final-step discipline is what separates lasting change from temporary theater

class_schedule.topic: Ethics, Diversity, and Inclusion in Organizations
learning_objectives: Apply ethical frameworks (utilitarian, deontological, virtue, justice) to managerial decisions
Recognize conditions that increase unethical behavior in organizations (pressure, rationalization, moral disengagement)
Distinguish diversity, inclusion, and belonging, and evaluate the evidence on their business effects
Design practices that produce substantive rather than performative diversity outcomes
readings: Robbins & Judge, Organizational Behavior (19th ed.), Ch. 2 - Diversity in Organizations, and Ch. 1 ethics sections
Bazerman & Tenbrunsel, Blind Spots (2011), Ch. 1-3
Edmondson & Mortensen, 'What Psychological Safety Looks Like in a Hybrid Workplace' (HBR, April 2021)
Kalev, Dobbin & Kelly, 'Best Practices or Best Guesses? Assessing the Efficacy of Corporate Affirmative Action and Diversity Policies' (American Sociological Review, 2006)

lecture_notes.introduction: When Ken Frazier, then CEO of Merck, resigned from President Trump's American Manufacturing Council in August 2017 after the Charlottesville rally, he became the first of a cascade of CEOs who followed. His statement was short: America's leaders must honor our fundamental values by clearly rejecting expressions of hatred, bigotry and group supremacy. The decision cost him nothing he needed and carried significant political risk, but it was the ethical decision according to Frazier's own stated values, and it became a model of principled leadership. Ethics in organizations is not usually that clear. Most ethical failures happen not because leaders choose to do wrong but because they fail to notice that an ethical choice is even in front of them. Max Bazerman and Ann Tenbrunsel call this bounded ethicality, and their research shows that otherwise honest people make unethical decisions under the same conditions that produce other cognitive errors: time pressure, motivated reasoning, gradual escalation, diffused responsibility, and the dilution of ethical cues in business language. The diversity and inclusion conversation is similarly easy to get wrong. Companies spend billions on DEI programs, yet meta-analyses show that most common interventions (mandatory diversity training, grievance procedures) produce either no effect or backlash. The interventions that work (structured mentorship, cross-functional task forces, explicit accountability, and removing bias from specific decisions) are less emotionally satisfying but actually change representation over time. This session treats ethics and inclusion as management problems that require the same evidence-based approach as other OB topics, not as matters of good intentions.
key_concepts: Ethical frameworks: utilitarianism (maximize overall welfare), deontology (duties and rights), virtue ethics (character and flourishing), justice (fairness in distribution and procedure); no single framework resolves all cases, and ethical reasoning often combines them
Bounded ethicality (Bazerman & Tenbrunsel): people systematically fail to recognize ethical dimensions of decisions and rationalize their behavior to preserve a self-image as ethical

Moral disengagement (Bandura): mechanisms that allow people to act against their own ethical standards without feeling guilty (euphemistic labeling, diffusion of responsibility, dehumanization, moral justification)
Ethical climate and culture: the shared perceptions of what is ethically correct behavior; culture that tolerates small ethical violations predicts larger ones over time
Conditions that increase unethical behavior: time pressure, competitive framing, authority compliance, gradual escalation, ambiguous accountability, financial incentives linked to narrow metrics
Diversity as representation: demographic characteristics of the workforce; the mere presence of diverse employees does not produce outcomes by itself
Inclusion as experience: the extent to which diverse employees feel valued, heard, and able to contribute authentically; inclusion mediates the relationship between diversity and performance
Belonging as identification: the extent to which employees feel they are part of the organizational identity; the deepest form of integration and the hardest to measure

Evidence on DEI interventions: mandatory diversity training has weak or negative effects; mentorship, structured promotion processes, accountability for representation, and diverse hiring slates have measurable positive effects

Structural versus attitudinal interventions: changing structures (who gets interviewed, who is on review panels, how performance is evaluated) outperforms changing attitudes (training, awareness) for moving representation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Ethics case exercise using the Volkswagen diesel emissions scandal. Walk through the timeline: engineering challenge (meet emissions standards at an unrealistic cost), decision point (install defeat devices to cheat tests), escalation (device deployed to millions of vehicles over a decade), discovery (academic researchers in West Virginia), consequences ($33 billion in fines, criminal prosecutions, brand damage). At each decision point, ask: who knew? Who could have stopped it? What pressures produced the decision? Use Bazerman and Tenbrunsel's bounded ethicality framework to analyze how individual engineers, middle managers, and executives could have seen themselves as ethical while participating in large-scale fraud. Discuss the mechanisms of moral disengagement (euphemism: software optimization; diffusion: not my area; justification: everyone is doing something similar). Then shift to the inclusion exercise: students analyze a real DEI program from a Fortune 500 company (using the Kalev, Dobbin, Kelly research on what works) and identify which interventions have evidence behind them and which are theater. Close with a commitment exercise: each student identifies one specific practice they will adopt in their next management role to make ethical considerations visible and to make inclusion substantive rather than symbolic.
setup_requirements: Volkswagen diesel scandal timeline and decision points
Bazerman & Tenbrunsel bounded ethicality framework handout
Kalev, Dobbin, Kelly effectiveness rankings for common DEI interventions
Fortune 500 DEI program samples for analysis

review.value: Large ethical collapses rarely start with a single big decision. They usually start with small rationalizations that compound over time, and each step is easier to take than the last. Leaders must build in friction at the small stages
Hiring diverse employees into an uninclusive culture produces turnover, not transformation. Inclusion is the work of making daily experience equitable, and it is structural more than attitudinal

review.key: Most Diversity Training Does Not Work
review.value: Meta-analyses show that mandatory diversity training is often ineffective or counterproductive. Structural interventions (mentorship, accountability, structured decisions) have measurable effects; training alone usually does not

review.key: Ethics Require Structures, Not Just Values
review.value: Stated values are necessary but not sufficient. Organizations with strong ethical outcomes have structures that make ethical considerations explicit, accountability mechanisms that work, and leaders who model the behavior under pressure

class_schedule.topic: Integration: Leadership in Practice and Personal Development
learning_objectives: Integrate individual, group, and organizational frameworks to diagnose complex leadership situations
Develop a personal leadership development plan based on strengths and growth areas
Apply deliberate practice principles to leadership skill building
Commit to a specific set of leadership behaviors to implement in your next role
readings: Northouse, Leadership: Theory and Practice (9th ed.), Ch. 16 - Leadership Ethics and final integration
Goleman, 'What Makes a Leader?' (Harvard Business Review, January 2004 reprint)
Ericsson & Pool, Peak: Secrets from the New Science of Expertise (2016), Ch. 1-3
Collins, Good to Great (2001), Ch. 2 - Level 5 Leadership

lecture_notes.introduction: Jim Collins's Good to Great identified a specific pattern among CEOs of companies that moved from average performance to sustained superiority. He called them Level 5 leaders: a paradoxical combination of personal humility and professional will, often quiet people who cared more about the company's success than their own recognition, who hired better people than themselves and credited them publicly, and who took personal responsibility for failures. Collins's list included Darwin Smith of Kimberly-Clark, Colman Mockler of Gillette, and others most students have never heard of, which was precisely his point. The charismatic CEO celebrated on magazine covers is not typically the leader who builds lasting excellence. This final session pulls together everything the course has covered and asks a harder question: what do you do with it? Leadership is not a class you pass; it is a practice you commit to. Like any practice, it improves only with deliberate effort, honest feedback, and sustained attention over years. Anders Ericsson's research on expertise gives us the most robust framework for building skill: deliberate practice requires a clear goal, feedback, and work at the edge of current ability, repeated over long periods. Applied to leadership, this means identifying the specific skills you most need to develop, finding feedback mechanisms (peers, mentors, 360-degree reviews, honest self-assessment), and putting in the repetitions. The alternative, which most managers default to, is accumulating experience without improvement. This session ends with a personal leadership development plan that you will implement and revisit in the final project. The course content is only useful if it becomes practice, and practice only leads to growth if it is deliberate.
key_concepts: Level 5 leadership (Collins): personal humility combined with professional will; self-effacing leaders who channel their ambition into the institution rather than themselves
Five levels of leadership (Collins): (1) highly capable individual, (2) contributing team member, (3) competent manager, (4) effective leader, (5) Level 5 executive; most leaders plateau at 3 or 4
Emotional intelligence (Goleman): self-awareness, self-regulation, motivation, empathy, social skill; meta-analytic support is modest but real, especially for senior leadership roles
Deliberate practice (Ericsson): focused, goal-directed practice at the edge of current ability, with immediate feedback and repetition; the mechanism underlying expertise in any field

The 10,000 hour rule misconception: Ericsson's point was not that 10,000 hours produces expertise, it was that 10,000 hours of deliberate practice, which most people never accumulate because casual experience does not count
360-degree feedback: collecting behavioral feedback from supervisors, peers, and direct reports; essential for self-awareness because most leaders overestimate their own effectiveness

Personal board of directors: a small group of mentors and honest critics who provide sustained guidance over years; research consistently finds that leaders who cultivate these relationships outperform those who do not

Leadership derailers (McCall): the qualities that make leaders successful early in their careers (drive, intelligence, ambition) can become derailers later if not balanced by humility, empathy, and capacity for change

Career stages and leadership: the leadership skills that matter shift as you rise; technical competence dominates early, human skills dominate middle, conceptual and political skills dominate senior roles
Sustained attention: leadership development takes years, not weeks; the research is consistent that the biggest predictor of improvement is whether the leader sustains deliberate attention over time
lecture_notes.demonstration: Personal leadership development plan workshop. Each student completes a structured reflection template in class: (1) Current strengths based on Big Five personality profile, 360-degree feedback if available, and honest self-assessment; (2) Current growth areas informed by specific feedback they have received or specific situations they have handled poorly; (3) Priority skill to develop in the next 12 months, chosen for leverage (high impact, possible to practice); (4) Deliberate practice plan including specific behaviors to adopt, feedback mechanisms, and a measurable outcome; (5) Personal board of directors list with at least three people the student will actively engage for mentorship or feedback. The instructor circulates, pressing students on specificity. A vague goal like be more assertive becomes in the next 60 days, I will speak first in the team meeting at least once, and I will name a disagreement directly rather than softening it. Students share their plans in pairs and commit to a 90-day check-in with their partner. Close with the course capstone question: having studied the research, what is the single most important thing you will do differently as a leader, and how will you know you did it?
setup_requirements: Personal development plan template (printed, five pages)
Big Five profile sheets from Week 2 for reference
Ericsson deliberate practice framework handout
90-day accountability partner pairing system

review.value: Twenty years of experience does not equal twenty years of development. Most managers plateau after a few years because they stop deliberately practicing. The leaders who keep growing are the ones who keep challenging themselves at the edge of their current ability

Self-awareness does not come from introspection alone; it comes from feedback. Leaders who do not actively seek honest feedback from peers, direct reports, and mentors are blind to their biggest growth opportunities
Collins's Level 5 leaders were not born with humility and will; they developed the combination over time, often through failure. The paradoxical blend is achievable for anyone who commits to the work

Leadership is a practice. Without sustained deliberate practice, even the best-educated leader will plateau. With it, even modestly talented leaders can reach unusual levels of effectiveness. The commitment to practice is the single decision that determines the trajectory

questions.question: A sales manager is frustrated with two new hires. Michael consistently hits his monthly quota but is often late to morning meetings and turns in reports with typos. Sarah attends every meeting, submits pristine reports, but has hit quota only once in four months. The manager concludes Sarah is more reliable and conscientious than Michael. Using the Big Five and evidence on job performance, explain what is wrong with this attribution, and describe what the manager should measure instead.
questions.answer:
The manager is confusing observable cues of conscientiousness (punctuality, report quality) with the underlying construct, which is specifically organized, responsible, and achievement-oriented behavior in the domain that matters.

Two errors:
1. Fundamental attribution error and trait overgeneralization: the manager is attributing Michael's lateness and typos to low conscientiousness in general, when it may reflect prioritization (he may be spending his early mornings cold-calling) or situational factors. Sarah's attention to meeting attendance and report quality may not reflect conscientiousness at all; it may reflect different motivations or different salience of visible versus outcome metrics.

2. Confusing process compliance with job performance: the evidence on personality and job performance (Barrick & Mount, 1991) is clear that conscientiousness predicts performance, but performance must be measured by outcomes, not by process compliance. In a sales role, the outcome is revenue. Michael is delivering the outcome; Sarah is not.

What the manager should measure:
- Actual sales outcomes: quota attainment, pipeline quality, customer retention, deal size
- Evidence of achievement striving: number of prospecting activities, follow-up rates, deal progression
- If the manager is concerned about the specific behaviors (punctuality, reports), address them directly as behaviors to correct rather than inferring personality traits

The manager's conclusion confuses visible effort with results. A high performer who is not following process should be coached on the process; a low performer who follows process should be coached on results. Flipping the labels (Sarah as more reliable) leads to rewarding the wrong thing and is one of the most common performance management errors in practice.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the fundamental attribution error and the confusion of visible cues with underlying traits
Identifies that performance must be measured by outcomes, not process compliance
Proposes specific, outcome-based measures for sales performance
Notes the practical implication that rewarding visible compliance over outcomes leads to worse hiring and coaching decisions

questions.question: Ron Johnson at JC Penney eliminated coupons in favor of everyday low prices, believing Apple Store principles would transfer. Identify three specific cognitive biases that contributed to this failure and describe a decision process that would have caught them.
questions.answer:
Three biases:

1. Fundamental attribution error: Johnson attributed Apple's retail success to his strategy (elegant design, no discounts, curated boutique experience) rather than to Apple's product ecosystem, iconic brand, and premium positioning. Applied to JC Penney customers (middle-income shoppers who bought on promotion), the same strategy produced different results because the product and customer were different.

2. Overconfidence: Johnson had a well-documented track record (Target, then Apple retail) and believed his instincts were reliable. He refused to pilot the changes in a subset of stores despite repeated recommendations from his team. Meta-analyses show executives routinely assign 90 percent confidence to predictions that come true 50 to 60 percent of the time.

3. Confirmation bias: Johnson surrounded himself with executives who supported the strategy and dismissed internal dissent from long-tenured JC Penney leaders as resistance to change. He read customer feedback selectively, treating positive responses as signal and negative responses as noise.

Decision process to catch the biases:

1. Pilot before rolling out: test the new strategy in 10 to 20 stores for 6 months, with rigorous measurement against control stores. This would have revealed the sales collapse before the full rollout. The Heath brothers call this ooching in Decisive.

2. Pre-mortem exercise: before launch, imagine the strategy has failed catastrophically and write the post-mortem. Ask why it failed. This activates the anti-confirmation-bias circuit and surfaces risks that the optimistic plan glosses over.

3. Red team: assign a small group the explicit job of finding reasons the strategy will fail, with protection from retaliation and direct access to the CEO. The team should be given real authority to surface dissent and present their case.

4. Decision rights and data triggers: commit in advance to specific metrics that would trigger rollback (for example, same-store sales declining by more than 5 percent in any single quarter). The precommitment counteracts the escalation of commitment that kept JC Penney pushing forward long after the signals were clear.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies three distinct biases with specific examples from the case
Proposes pilot testing or ooching as a process safeguard
Proposes pre-mortem, red team, or similar dissent-surfacing mechanism
Proposes pre-committed rollback criteria or data triggers

questions.question: Define psychological safety in precisely the sense Amy Edmondson uses, and explain how it differs from trust and from comfort. Why did Google's Project Aristotle find it was the most predictive factor of team effectiveness?
questions.answer:
Psychological safety (Edmondson, 1999): a shared belief held by members of a team that the team is safe for interpersonal risk taking. The key word is shared: it is a collective property of the team, not a description of each individual's feeling. It captures the belief that speaking up with ideas, questions, concerns, or mistakes will not be met with punishment or humiliation.

Difference from trust:
- Trust is a one-to-one property: I trust a specific person. Psychological safety is a group property: the group is safe to speak up.
- Trust usually involves confidence in another's ability, benevolence, or integrity; safety involves confidence that honest speech will not be punished.
- A team can have high trust in particular pairs and low psychological safety overall (for example, if the boss is supportive one-on-one but punishes dissent in meetings).

Difference from comfort:
- Comfort means the absence of tension or disagreement. Safety does NOT mean comfort.
- Psychologically safe teams actually have MORE disagreement, not less, because members feel free to challenge ideas and push back.
- The confusion of safety with comfort is common and produces teams that are polite, silent, and wrong.

Why Project Aristotle found it was most predictive: Google studied 180 teams and measured dozens of composition variables (average IQ, extroversion, tenure, friendship, demographics). Almost nothing predicted performance. What did predict performance was how teams worked together, and psychological safety was the top factor. The explanation is that high-performing work today is knowledge work: it requires surfacing problems, asking for help, admitting ignorance, and proposing half-formed ideas. Teams that punish these behaviors (even subtly) lose the information they need to improve. Teams that reward them aggregate more knowledge and catch more errors. In an environment where the task is complex and the answers are not pre-known, safety is the enabling condition for the team to actually think together.

rubric.key: Accurately defines psychological safety with the shared belief and interpersonal risk components
Correctly distinguishes safety from trust (group vs one-to-one) and from comfort (safety is not absence of conflict)
Explains why Project Aristotle found it more predictive than composition (knowledge work requires information aggregation)

questions.question: A call center's quality assurance team is struggling. They review customer service calls and provide feedback to representatives, but turnover is 80 percent per year. Scores on engagement surveys are low on autonomy, feedback, and meaningfulness. Apply the Job Characteristics Model (Hackman & Oldham) to redesign the job. Specify current dimensions, the Motivating Potential Score trajectory, and at least four specific redesign moves.
questions.answer:
Current JCM scoring (1-7 scale):
- Skill variety: 3 (listening to calls, filling out rubrics, writing feedback; limited variety)
- Task identity: 4 (complete task per call but disconnected from outcome)
- Task significance: 4 (matters to someone but not visible)
- Autonomy: 2 (prescribed rubric, prescribed feedback templates, no discretion)
- Feedback from the job itself: 2 (scores are submitted, no visible outcome)

MPS = ((3 + 4 + 4) / 3) * 2 * 2 = 3.67 * 2 * 2 = 14.67 (very low)

Redesign moves:

1. Form natural work units (task identity): assign each QA specialist to a specific team of reps rather than a random queue of calls. The QA specialist owns the development of their team of reps over time. Task identity rises to 6.

2. Establish client relationships (task significance): QA specialists have a direct feedback loop with the reps they review, including weekly one-on-one coaching sessions. They can see the rep improve over time. Significance rises to 6.

3. Vertical loading (autonomy): remove the standard rubric requirement and train QA specialists to exercise judgment about what to call out in each call. Allow them to customize feedback templates and to experiment with different coaching approaches. Autonomy rises to 5.

4. Open feedback channels (feedback from the job): connect the QA system to downstream customer satisfaction scores and rep retention metrics. The QA specialist sees whether the reps they coached improved, whether customer complaints decreased, and whether reps they supported stayed in the job longer. Feedback rises to 6.

5. Combining tasks (skill variety): the QA role also takes on new-hire training design for the team they support, giving them a second dimension of work and allowing them to apply what they learn from call review to the training curriculum. Skill variety rises to 6.

New MPS = ((6 + 6 + 6) / 3) * 5 * 6 = 6 * 5 * 6 = 180 (high)

The redesign turns the QA role from a low-autonomy compliance function into a coaching and development role with visible outcomes. Research from USAA and Mayo Clinic call centers shows that this kind of redesign reduces turnover dramatically and improves downstream customer outcomes, even though it requires higher pay and longer training.

rubric.key: Correct initial JCM scoring with at least approximately correct MPS calculation
Four distinct, specific redesign moves mapped to Hackman and Oldham's implementing concepts
Correct post-redesign MPS calculation showing substantial improvement
Acknowledgment of trade-offs (higher pay, more training) and connection to real-world evidence

questions.question: An engineering manager wants to introduce a quarterly bonus tied to individual story points completed. Using expectancy theory and evidence on extrinsic rewards, predict the likely consequences and recommend an alternative.
questions.answer:
Expectancy theory prediction (Vroom: M = E x I x V):

1. Expectancy (effort to performance): unclear. Story points are a team-estimated unit and are gamed by engineers adjusting their estimates upward. If points are not a valid measure of performance, the E link is weak.

2. Instrumentality (performance to reward): high. The bonus is directly tied to points delivered, so completing more points produces a larger bonus.

3. Valence (reward value): mixed. For some engineers, the bonus is meaningful. For others (senior engineers already paid well), the marginal value is low.

Expectancy theory predicts that motivation will be variable and the behavior change will be directed at what actually affects the metric (story points), not necessarily at what benefits the team.

Likely consequences:

1. Gaming: engineers will inflate story point estimates, pick up the cheap tickets that look high but take little effort, and avoid complex technical debt that takes time without generating points.

2. Decline in collaboration: helping a colleague finish their tickets costs you your own story points. Code review quality will drop. Pair programming will stop.

3. Decline in code quality: the fastest way to complete points is to skip tests, defer refactoring, and take shortcuts. Quality degrades over 6 to 12 months.

4. Decline in intrinsic motivation (Deci & Ryan): engineers who previously cared about craft now focus on the metric. Over time, the intrinsic motivation that produced the high-quality work in the first place is crowded out by extrinsic focus.

5. Turnover of the best engineers: the engineers who cared most about quality and collaboration will be dissatisfied and leave. The engineers who remain are the ones who game effectively.

Alternative recommendation:

1. Pay fairly and generously at base: conscientious professionals do their best work when they trust they are paid fairly and do not need to focus on compensation. Benchmark against market and pay at or above the 75th percentile.

2. Recognize at the team level: celebrate team accomplishments (shipping a major feature, closing a set of bugs, reducing incidents) rather than individual throughput. Recognition can be public and specific without being financial.

3. Give autonomy: within clear priorities, let engineers choose how to work, what to pair on, and how to organize their time. Autonomy is a major intrinsic motivator for technical professionals.

4. Clear specific team goals (Locke & Latham): instead of counting individual story points, set specific difficult team goals tied to outcomes (ship feature X by Y date, reduce incident rate to Z). Provide ongoing feedback against those goals.

5. Reward growth: pay increases tied to technical growth and contribution to the team (mentorship, shipping hard things, improving the codebase) rather than to a narrow individual metric.

This approach matches the evidence on extrinsic rewards for creative complex work: base pay matters, individual incentives crowd out intrinsic motivation and produce gaming, and team-level autonomy plus specific challenging goals produce better outcomes than individual bonuses.

rubric.key: Correct application of expectancy theory with analysis of E, I, and V
Identifies specific likely consequences (gaming, decline in collaboration, decline in quality)
Alternative recommendation grounded in SDT, goal-setting, or team-level incentives

questions.question: A team has been working together for six months. They appear productive on the surface, but the manager notices that decisions are never challenged in meetings, mistakes are hidden, and junior members never speak up. Diagnose the problem in terms of psychological safety and propose three specific interventions to improve it.
questions.answer:
Diagnosis: the team has low psychological safety. The symptoms (no challenge, hidden mistakes, silent juniors) are the classic Edmondson indicators. The team is likely producing below its potential because members do not feel safe taking interpersonal risks, which means the information needed for decision quality and error correction is not being surfaced.

Three specific interventions:

1. Frame the work as learning, not execution. The manager should explicitly reframe the team's identity: we are doing novel work, we will make mistakes, and our job is to catch them early. Make it public and repeat it. This is Edmondson's setting the stage intervention.

2. Model vulnerability from the top. The manager should deliberately admit their own mistakes in team meetings, share things they do not know, and publicly change their mind when a team member raises a good point. The behavior the manager models becomes the norm, especially for junior members watching for cues about what is safe.

3. Invite participation structurally. Direct questions to specific team members (especially the silent ones) in a way that makes speaking up easy: Sarah, you have worked on similar systems, what are we missing? Use silent brainstorming or written input before open discussion to prevent extroverts and seniors from dominating. Rotate meeting facilitation so that junior members have explicit authority to call on colleagues.

Optional fourth: institute a no-blame post-mortem process for mistakes, where the team debriefs failures with explicit focus on system causes rather than individual fault. This is what the aviation industry adopted with the Crew Resource Management model and what high-reliability organizations use to surface errors.

Measurement: administer the Fearless Organization Scan (Edmondson's instrument) before and after the interventions, quarterly. Track specific behaviors: number of disagreements voiced in meetings, number of mistakes reported, and whether junior members are speaking without being directly invited.

rubric.key: Correct diagnosis connecting symptoms to low psychological safety
Three distinct, specific interventions targeting different mechanisms
Includes measurement or accountability for whether the interventions are working

questions.question: Your direct report, Priya, has been missing deadlines for the past three weeks. Her work is technically excellent when it lands, but the delays are causing downstream problems for the team. Draft an SBI (Situation-Behavior-Impact) feedback statement and explain why each element matters.
questions.answer:
SBI draft:

Situation: In the past three weeks, I have noticed a pattern with your project deliverables. Specifically, the database schema you committed to deliver by Monday arrived on Thursday, the API contract due last Tuesday arrived on Friday, and the load testing report due this Monday has not been delivered yet.

Behavior: The specific behavior I am describing is missing the delivery dates we agreed to in sprint planning. Your work, when it arrives, is technically excellent, so this is not about quality. It is specifically about the delivery dates.

Impact: The impact on the team has been significant. The front-end team had to delay their integration work twice, the load testing team had to reschedule their environment, and I had to push back the sprint demo with the stakeholders. This is affecting the team's trust in our commitments, and it is affecting your reputation because colleagues are starting to plan around expected delays.

Follow-up (open the conversation, not close it): I want to understand what is happening on your end. Is something getting in the way that I am not seeing? Is the estimation process wrong? Is there something personal going on that I should know about?

Why each element matters:

- Situation: specific, verifiable, dated. Avoids you always and you never which are defensive triggers and also rarely true. Three concrete examples establish the pattern without accusation.

- Behavior: focuses on observable behavior rather than a trait inference. Saying you are unreliable is a trait attribution that produces defensiveness; saying you missed delivery dates is a behavioral observation that Priya can actually respond to.

- Impact: translates the behavior into specific consequences. Without impact, the feedback can feel arbitrary. With impact, Priya understands why the behavior matters, not just that her manager dislikes it.

- Follow-up question: opens the conversation to information the manager does not have. Maybe Priya is dealing with a family situation, maybe the estimation process is broken, maybe she is blocked by something upstream. Without the question, the manager is delivering a verdict rather than having a conversation. The research on high-quality conversations (Gallup, Microsoft) shows that feedback accompanied by genuine curiosity produces behavior change, while feedback delivered as verdict produces compliance at best and defensive withdrawal at worst.

rubric.key: Specific dated situation examples rather than general statements
Observable behavior separated from trait attribution
Concrete impact on team, stakeholders, or individual reputation
Follow-up question that opens the conversation to diagnostic information

questions.question: You are negotiating with a vendor for a three-year software contract. Their opening quote is $450,000 per year. Your budget cap is $350,000 per year, and your alternative vendor would cost $400,000 per year with weaker features. Using the Harvard Negotiation Project framework, identify your BATNA, reservation price, and ZOPA, then describe your preparation strategy and two integrative moves you could propose to bridge the gap.
questions.answer:
Framework analysis:

1. BATNA: the alternative vendor at $400,000 per year. You can walk away and sign with them if this negotiation fails. The BATNA is your fallback, and it defines the floor of what you are willing to accept here.

2. Reservation price: the most you will pay for this vendor before walking to the BATNA. Technically it is $400,000 because above that, the alternative vendor is equally expensive with weaker features, so you might still prefer this vendor for the features. Realistically, since your budget cap is $350,000, the reservation price is $350,000 (budget is a hard constraint).

3. ZOPA (Zone of Possible Agreement): the overlap between their minimum and your maximum. You do not know their minimum, but given that the alternative vendor charges $400,000 and that vendors typically have margin above their floor, their minimum is likely somewhere between $250,000 and $400,000. Your maximum is $350,000. The ZOPA is probably the range $250,000 to $350,000. No deal is possible above $350,000, so the gap from their opening ($450,000) to your cap ($350,000) is $100,000 per year that must be closed.

Preparation strategy:

1. Anchor their estimate of your willingness to pay. Do not react to their $450,000 with counter-anchoring too close to your target; that signals flexibility. Provide a principled counter-anchor with justification: Based on our budget and the comparable alternative at $400,000, we are looking at the $300,000 range for a three-year commitment.

2. Identify their interests beyond price: long-term customer (they benefit from multi-year retention), case study and reference (they benefit from your logo and case study), product roadmap input (they may value a design partner), expansion potential (they may value future upsell).

3. Identify your interests beyond price: feature completeness, implementation support, contract flexibility, service level agreements, audit rights, termination rights, ability to add seats later without renegotiating.

Two integrative moves:

1. Longer commitment for lower annual price. Propose a 4 or 5 year agreement at $330,000 per year with auto-renewal and a price lock. This trades something they value (predictable revenue, reduced churn risk, lower acquisition cost for year 4 and 5) for something you value (lower annual spend within budget).

2. Marketing and case study partnership. Offer to be a named reference customer, participate in a case study, and speak at their annual conference. Vendor marketing and sales teams place significant value on customer references (the cost of acquiring new customers without them is much higher). This can unlock another $20,000 to $50,000 per year in discount that their pricing team could not give on price alone, because it converts the discount into a marketing expense on their side.

Combined with a longer commitment, these moves can realistically close the gap to $340,000 per year with a multi-year term, bringing the deal inside your budget with features your alternative vendor cannot match. The key insight is that the negotiation that looks zero-sum on price can be positive-sum on the full set of interests, and the prepared negotiator spends most of the value by identifying those interests before the conversation starts.

rubric.key: Correct identification of BATNA and reservation price with justification
Correct ZOPA analysis with reasoning about the vendor's likely floor
Preparation strategy including counter-anchoring and interest identification
Two distinct integrative moves that create value beyond the price dimension

questions.question: Jeffrey Pfeffer argues that people who refuse to engage with organizational politics lose to people who engage. Is that a cynical view or an accurate view? Use the French and Raven bases of power to argue both sides.
questions.answer:
Pfeffer's observation is accurate, and calling it cynical misses the point. The question is whether engaging with politics means manipulating or building coalitions, and those are different things.

Argument that it is accurate:

1. Power is a relationship (Emerson, French and Raven). Even the most competent individual contributor cannot get resources, prioritize projects, or protect their team from interference without understanding where power comes from and how to acquire it. Refusing to engage with politics means refusing to participate in the mechanism through which organizational decisions actually get made.

2. Position power alone is not enough. French and Raven's research shows that legitimate, reward, and coercive power (the position bases) produce compliance but not commitment. To build expert power and referent power, you have to invest in relationships, communicate your competence, and cultivate visibility. That looks like politics to someone who believes good work should speak for itself, but it is actually how expert and referent power are built.

3. The people who refuse to engage typically lose the coalition-building battle and then blame the winners for being political. Pfeffer's studies show this pattern repeatedly: the talented technical lead who does not promote their work, does not maintain relationships with the executive team, and does not publicly claim credit gets passed over for a less competent peer who does all three. The technical lead then concludes the organization is unfair. Pfeffer's response: the organization was playing by a well-known set of rules that the technical lead chose not to play by.

Argument that it is cynical (the push-back):

1. Some forms of political engagement are genuinely corrupt: taking credit for others' work, undermining colleagues, backstabbing, cultivating relationships for their instrumental value only, lying in meetings. Pfeffer sometimes describes these as effective, which collapses effective into good.

2. The cultural baggage of politics conflates two different things: building coalitions for legitimate purposes and scheming for personal advantage. Telling people to engage with politics without distinguishing can encourage the second type.

3. Organizations that reward pure political behavior (Uber circa 2016, Wells Fargo circa 2015) are pathological, and Pfeffer's advice to play the game as it is played can reinforce the pathology rather than changing it.

Synthesis: Pfeffer is right about the descriptive claim (people who refuse to engage lose) and usually right about the prescriptive claim (you should engage), but the right way to engage is to build personal power through expertise and relationships, advocate openly for your work and team, and build coalitions for purposes you can defend publicly. The line sits at whether your political engagement would embarrass you if disclosed. If not, it is coalition building; if yes, it is scheming. The French and Raven framework supports this: the personal power bases (expert, referent) are built through behaviors that are ethically defensible, and they produce more lasting influence than the position power bases that require scheming to maintain.

rubric.key: Accurate application of French and Raven's five bases to the question
Strong argument that Pfeffer is accurate, grounded in how power actually works
Honest engagement with the cynical-reading critique (conflation of coalition-building with scheming)
Synthesis or principled distinction between ethical engagement and corrupt politics

questions.question: Compare Bass's transformational leadership (the four I's) with Greenleaf's servant leadership. Identify two significant overlaps and two significant differences. Which framework better fits Satya Nadella's leadership of Microsoft? Justify with evidence.
questions.answer:
Overlaps:

1. Focus on follower development. Both frameworks move beyond transactional exchange and argue that leaders should develop followers, not just direct them. Transformational leadership's individualized consideration and servant leadership's helping subordinates grow are nearly identical behaviors; both emphasize coaching, attention to individual development, and long-term investment in people.

2. Emphasis on values and meaning. Both frameworks argue that leadership is about connecting work to a larger purpose and that values should guide leader behavior. Bass's idealized influence and Greenleaf's ethical behavior and community creation both place values at the center of leadership rather than treating them as peripheral.

Differences:

1. Source of vision. Transformational leadership has the leader set a vision and inspire followers to adopt it; the leader is the source of direction. Servant leadership inverts this: the leader's job is to serve the followers' needs and the community's wellbeing, and vision emerges from that service rather than being imposed from above.

2. Test of effectiveness. Transformational leadership's test is whether followers produce extraordinary effort and whether organizational performance improves. Servant leadership's test, in Greenleaf's own words, is whether those served become healthier, wiser, freer, more autonomous, and more likely themselves to become servants. These tests can produce different judgments of the same leader: a transformational leader who achieves results through draining demands might pass the transformational test but fail the servant test.

Which fits Nadella better:

Nadella is closer to a blend of both, but if forced to choose, servant leadership is probably the better fit. Evidence:

1. Growth mindset framing (Dweck). Nadella's first all-hands memo in 2014 framed the transformation around a concept (learn-it-all over know-it-all) that centered employee growth, not his vision. He did not ask Microsoft to follow him; he asked Microsoft to invest in itself.

2. Empathy as an explicit leadership requirement. Nadella has repeatedly discussed empathy, and he has described it as the single skill he most deliberately practices. Servant leadership makes empathy central; transformational leadership treats it as supportive.

3. Emphasis on customer and employee wellbeing as ends, not means. Nadella's public statements consistently position Microsoft's purpose as empowering every person and every organization on the planet to achieve more. The language is servant-oriented: the purpose is the empowerment of others, with Microsoft's success as a consequence rather than the goal.

4. Willingness to reverse predecessor's decisions by giving credit. Nadella killed stack ranking, apologized for comments women in tech found offensive, and publicly gave credit to Bill Gates and Steve Ballmer. Transformational leaders tend to position themselves as the vision-setter in contrast to the past; Nadella deliberately avoided that positioning.

He also has transformational elements (the vision of mobile-first, cloud-first; the bold acquisitions of LinkedIn and GitHub; the public framing of a new Microsoft), so the honest answer is that Nadella draws from both. But the distinctive feature of his leadership, the one that distinguishes him from other successful tech CEOs, is the servant orientation.

rubric.key: Two clearly articulated overlaps between the frameworks
Two clearly articulated differences between the frameworks
Argued choice of which fits Nadella with specific evidence (at least three pieces)
Acknowledgment that Nadella is a blend and the answer is not black and white

questions.question: A technology company has collaborative and innovative in its stated values, but the actual promotion criteria reward individual star performance, senior leaders take credit for others' work in public meetings, and new hires are told privately that you have to fight for your ideas around here. Using Schein's three-level model, diagnose the culture and explain why announcing a new values statement will not change anything.
questions.answer:
Schein's three-level analysis:

1. Artifacts (visible): values posters, collaborative and innovative branding in recruiting materials, open-plan office, team-building events, employee handbook language. These are visible but misleading because they reflect what the company wants to say, not what it does.

2. Espoused values (stated): collaboration and innovation. Leadership statements, onboarding materials, and external communications all emphasize these values. Employees can quote the values from memory.

3. Underlying assumptions (actual drivers): individual achievement is what gets rewarded, power flows from claiming visibility for ideas, trust between peers is dangerous because peers become competitors, the real test of belonging is willingness to fight for your own advancement. These assumptions contradict the espoused values but they are what drive actual behavior.

Diagnosis: the company has a classic values-behavior gap. The espoused values and the underlying assumptions are in conflict, and the underlying assumptions are winning. This is visible in three signals the prompt describes: promotion criteria reward individual performance (underlying assumption operationalized in the formal system), leaders take credit publicly (underlying assumption modeled from the top), and new hires are coached to fight for ideas (underlying assumption transmitted through socialization).

Why a new values statement will not change anything:

1. Announcing values does not alter the promotion criteria. As long as promotions reward individual star performance, ambitious employees will continue to optimize for individual visibility, regardless of what the values statement says. The underlying assumption is reinforced by the reward system, which is a structural force much stronger than a poster.

2. Announcing values does not alter leader behavior. If senior leaders continue to take credit for others' work in public, the new values statement is received as hypocrisy by employees, which damages trust further. Values statements that conflict with modeled behavior are worse than no statement, because they teach employees that the company's stated values are cover.

3. Socialization transmits the actual culture, not the stated culture. New hires learn what is true within the first three months, and they learn it from watching who gets promoted, who gets fired, and what their manager rewards day to day. You have to fight for your ideas around here is the real onboarding message, and it will continue to be until the reward system changes.

4. Schein's model predicts that culture change must happen at the level of underlying assumptions, which requires changing the structures that reinforce them (hiring criteria, promotion criteria, reward systems, performance reviews, leader modeling). Announcing values addresses the level of espoused values without touching the level that actually drives behavior.

What would actually work: rewrite the promotion criteria to include collaborative contribution (explicitly measurable via peer reviews or contribution to team outcomes), hold senior leaders accountable for credit-sharing (with specific documented examples in their performance reviews), train managers to coach new hires in collaborative norms, and sustain all of this for several years. Microsoft's cultural change under Nadella is the model case: it took years of structural work, not a new values statement.

rubric.key: Correct application of Schein's three levels with specific evidence from the prompt
Clear diagnosis of the values-behavior gap and why it is the critical issue
Three or more reasons why announcing values will not change anything, grounded in Schein's framework
Specific structural interventions that would actually change the underlying assumptions

questions.question: Hersey-Blanchard's Situational Leadership model tells managers to adapt their style to follower readiness. The empirical support for the specific prescriptions is mixed. Is the model still useful? Argue yes or no and defend your position.
questions.answer:
Yes, the model is still useful, but with an important caveat: it should be treated as a diagnostic heuristic, not as a precise prescription.

Why the model is useful:

1. The central insight is robust and supported by other evidence. The claim that leaders should adapt their behavior to the follower's ability and willingness is consistent with Path-Goal theory, with contingency research more broadly, and with everyday experience. A new hire needs different support than a veteran expert, and applying the same directive style to both is a common and costly mistake.

2. The model forces managers to diagnose before prescribing. Before using it, the manager has to think specifically about what each team member knows, what they can do, and how committed they are. That diagnosis alone is valuable, even if the subsequent style selection is imprecise.

3. It provides a shared vocabulary. When a manager and a direct report can agree that we are in a D2 situation here (competent but uncommitted), they can have a productive conversation about what support is needed. Shared vocabulary reduces ambiguity in management relationships.

4. It is better than the alternatives most managers default to. The typical default is either consistent style (always directive or always laissez-faire) or mood-driven style. Situational Leadership, even applied loosely, is an improvement over these alternatives because it at least asks the right question.

The caveat:

1. The specific prescriptions (the quadrants and the D1-D4 to S1-S4 mapping) are not well supported empirically. Managers who use the model rigidly, following the prescriptions without judgment, will sometimes apply the wrong style to a person who does not fit the model's categories.

2. The model oversimplifies. Real followers do not move linearly through readiness levels. A person can be D4 on one task and D1 on another, depending on the task. The model is best applied task by task, not person by person.

3. Readiness is not always a fixed property of the follower. Sometimes low readiness is a symptom of a problem the leader created (inadequate context, unclear expectations, lack of support). Fixing the leader's behavior should sometimes precede adjusting style to the follower's apparent readiness.

Position: the model is useful as a diagnostic aid and a conversation tool, provided the manager holds it loosely and combines it with specific knowledge about the person and the task. It should not be used as a recipe. Treated this way, it passes the test of being more useful than the alternatives most managers default to, which is the practical standard for whether a tool deserves to stay in the toolkit.

rubric.key: Clear yes or no position with defense
Multiple reasons the model is useful beyond specific prescriptions
Honest acknowledgment of the empirical limits
Principled distinction between the core insight and the specific prescriptions

questions.question: A mid-sized hospital is trying to shift its safety culture. Despite posters, mandatory training, and a new reporting system, nurses still hesitate to report medication errors because they fear blame. The CEO wants to know why her initiatives are not working. Apply Kotter's 8-step model to diagnose what is missing and propose the three most important corrections.
questions.answer:
Diagnosis using Kotter's 8-step model:

1. Urgency (Step 1): unclear. The CEO knows the culture is a problem, but it is not obvious that the frontline nurses feel urgent about changing it. Without shared urgency, posters and training are treated as corporate theater. Symptom: nurses are not reporting because they do not feel the change is a priority for their immediate leadership.

2. Guiding coalition (Step 2): likely missing. The prompt mentions the CEO wants but does not describe a coalition that includes nurse managers, respected frontline nurses, pharmacists, and physicians. A CEO alone cannot change a safety culture in a hospital; the change must be carried by a cross-functional coalition with credibility at the unit level.

3. Vision (Step 3): may exist but unclear. Better safety culture is not a vision; it is a goal. A vision must be specific enough to guide behavior: A culture where every safety concern, no matter how small, is reported and investigated with learning as the priority and blame off the table.

4. Communication (Step 4): weak. Posters are a low-bandwidth channel that communicates exactly the wrong message (we care about looking safe). Effective communication would include frequent direct conversations between leaders and staff, storytelling about real cases where reporting led to improvement, and visible follow-through on reports.

5. Empowerment (Step 5): missing. The new reporting system is a tool, but the nurses still fear blame, which means the empowerment to report without consequences has not actually been granted. The structural obstacles (the blame culture, the managers who punish reports) are in place.

6. Short-term wins (Step 6): unclear. The prompt does not mention any. Without visible wins from reporting (a small error caught, an improvement made, a public thank-you to a nurse who reported), the change has no momentum.

7. Consolidation (Step 7): not applicable yet.

8. Anchoring in culture (Step 8): impossible until the earlier steps work.

Three most important corrections:

1. Fix the blame problem directly and publicly. The nurses are not hesitating because they do not know how to report; they are hesitating because they expect blame. The CEO must identify specific managers who have punished reports and hold them accountable, then celebrate publicly a case where a nurse reported a serious error and was supported. This is empowerment in Kotter's sense: removing the obstacle that prevents the new behavior. Without this move, no number of posters will help.

2. Build the real guiding coalition. The CEO should recruit a cross-functional change team that includes respected nurse managers, a senior physician champion, a pharmacist, a patient safety officer, and two or three frontline nurses with strong peer credibility. The coalition leads the change, not the CEO alone. Their credibility is what moves the culture at the unit level, because the nurses are watching their direct peers, not the CEO.

3. Generate visible short-term wins. In the first 90 days, identify three specific cases where a reported error was investigated, a system change was made, and the nurse was publicly recognized. Communicate these cases widely through the hospital. Do not let the change be abstract; show concrete examples of the new pattern. This is how Alan Mulally's Ford turnaround built momentum (his first red moment with Mark Fields was precisely this kind of visible short-term win).

Optional fourth correction: measure and publish. The CEO should commit to publishing the monthly count of reported incidents, with the explicit framing that more reports are a good sign (more catching of errors), not a bad sign. This reframes the metric and protects the change from being undermined by anyone who would use the reports as evidence of problems to be hidden.

rubric.key: Correct application of at least four of Kotter's steps to diagnose the situation
Identification of the blame problem as the empowerment obstacle that blocks everything else
Coalition building specifically at the unit and frontline level, not just executive
Concrete short-term wins strategy with a visible mechanism for reinforcement

questions.question: Kalev, Dobbin & Kelly's 2006 research found that many common DEI interventions (mandatory diversity training, grievance procedures, diversity evaluations) have weak or negative effects on representation, while other interventions (mentoring programs, diversity task forces, explicit accountability for managers) have measurable positive effects. Why do the interventions that sound the most rigorous often fail, and what principle can we use to distinguish effective from ineffective interventions?
questions.answer:
Why the apparently rigorous interventions fail:

1. Mandatory diversity training triggers resistance. Research consistently shows that when people are required to attend training that they experience as accusatory, they react with reactance (psychological resistance), and the training produces attitudes opposite to the intended effect. Voluntary training does better because the people who attend are receptive; mandatory training does worse because the people who most need it are not.

2. Grievance procedures centralize and individualize the problem. They require the victim to come forward, bear the cost of reporting, and trust a system that often protects the organization more than the complainant. Research shows they are used infrequently and that filers often face retaliation, making them a weak lever for systemic change.

3. Diversity performance evaluations can be gamed. If a manager is evaluated on whether they care about diversity, the response is performative signaling rather than structural change. The evaluation measures the wrong thing (attitudes and stated commitment) rather than the outcome (representation changes).

Why the effective interventions work:

1. Mentoring programs create structural access. Underrepresented employees typically lack informal access to senior leaders, which is where career advice, high-visibility assignments, and sponsorship happen. Formal mentoring programs give them the access, and the mentor relationships produce sponsorship (active advocacy for promotion), not just advice.

2. Diversity task forces distribute responsibility. They create a cross-functional group with explicit accountability for making specific changes, and they give line managers visible responsibility. Task forces are local, specific, and outcome-oriented, which avoids the generalized resistance of mandatory training.

3. Accountability for managers shifts what they optimize for. If a manager's performance review includes specific outcome metrics (diverse interview slates, diverse hires, retention of underrepresented employees), the manager changes behavior to affect the metric. This is behavioral accountability rather than attitudinal change, and it scales.

Principle: interventions that target structures and outcomes (mentoring access, hiring slates, manager accountability, promotion processes) tend to work. Interventions that target attitudes or rely on voluntary effort (training without practice, grievance procedures requiring victim action, evaluations of caring) tend to fail. This is the structural vs attitudinal principle: behavior changes structures, not the other way around. It is consistent with broader evidence in OB, including the evidence on culture change (rewards and promotion criteria change culture, values statements do not), on debiasing (processes debias people, awareness rarely does), and on motivation (goal-setting and feedback change performance, pep talks rarely do).

Implication for DEI strategy: measure representation and retention by specific demographic groups, hold managers accountable for their unit's numbers, remove bias from specific decisions (blind resume review, structured interviews, diverse interview panels), and invest in sponsorship and mentorship programs. Deprioritize broad attitudinal training as a primary lever, though it may have a role as supplemental.

rubric.key: Explains at least two reasons why common interventions fail (reactance, performativity, low-power victim reporting)
Explains why the effective interventions work through structural mechanisms
Clear articulation of the structural vs attitudinal principle as the distinguishing criterion

questions.question: At the end of the course, write a 300-word leadership development plan that identifies one strength to leverage, one growth area, a specific deliberate-practice goal for the next 90 days, and the feedback mechanism you will use to know whether you improved. Ground it in the frameworks and evidence covered in this course.
questions.answer:
A strong answer will vary by student but should include all four required elements and apply course concepts correctly.

Exemplar answer:

Strength to leverage (Big Five and Goleman, Week 2 and 15):
My Big Five profile shows high conscientiousness (88th percentile) and moderate emotional stability. I deliver on commitments and stay calm under pressure. This is useful in the project management dimension of leadership: when I say I will do something, it happens, and when the team is stressed I can provide a stable presence.

Growth area (Week 7 feedback and Week 8 power):
I avoid direct difficult conversations. In my team, I tend toward ruinous empathy in Kim Scott's framework: I care personally but do not challenge directly. In my last 360 review, two peers described me as too diplomatic which I now recognize as a euphemism for you do not tell us what we need to hear. This also limits my ability to build expert and referent power (French and Raven) because I withhold the candor that would make my voice matter in tough decisions.

90-day deliberate practice goal (Ericsson, Week 15):
In the next 90 days, I will practice giving direct feedback using the SBI model at least twice per week. Specifically, in my weekly one-on-ones with my three direct reports, I will deliver at least one piece of specific behavioral feedback per report (SBI format, starting with impact on the team or the work). I will keep a log of each instance with the situation, how the conversation went, and what I noticed afterward.

Feedback mechanism (Week 7 and Week 15):
I will ask my two peer mentors to complete a short three-item survey at the 45-day and 90-day marks: (1) Has the clarity of feedback to you or about team decisions changed in the past six weeks? (2) Can you give me a specific example? (3) What is still missing? I will share the results with my manager and incorporate the observations into my next quarter's plan. The 90-day metric of success is at least one piece of concrete observable evidence that my directness has improved, as judged by peers I have not coached on what to say.

This plan grounds personal development in the course's evidence: it uses specific behaviors rather than vague intentions (goal-setting theory), it uses deliberate practice (Ericsson), it uses feedback rather than self-report (avoiding the overconfidence bias), and it targets a specific skill at the edge of current ability.

rubric.key: Specific strength with evidence (not just I am hard-working)
Specific growth area grounded in course frameworks or real feedback
Concrete deliberate-practice goal with repetition schedule and measurable behavior
Honest feedback mechanism that does not rely on the student's self-assessment

major_assignments.title: Leadership Case Analysis: Diagnosing a Real Turnaround
major_assignments.description: Conduct a rigorous case analysis of a real organizational turnaround or failure using the individual, group, and organizational frameworks covered in the first six weeks of the course. You will select one of five approved cases (Microsoft under Nadella, Ford under Mulally, GM under Barra, JC Penney under Johnson, Theranos under Holmes) and apply the course frameworks to explain what happened and why. The analysis is not a retelling of the case; it is a diagnostic document that identifies the specific mechanisms that produced the outcomes, grounds each claim in evidence, and concludes with three specific leadership lessons that would be useful to a new manager facing similar challenges.
objectives: Apply Big Five personality research, motivation theories, and team effectiveness findings to explain real organizational outcomes
Integrate individual, group, and organizational levels of analysis in a coherent diagnosis
Distinguish evidence-based claims from speculation and acknowledge the limits of case analysis
Produce a professional-quality written analysis with clear structure, specific evidence, and defensible conclusions
deliverables: Written case analysis (8 to 10 pages, single-spaced, 11-point font) with sections for case background, individual-level analysis, group-level analysis, organizational-level analysis, integrated diagnosis, and leadership lessons
Evidence appendix citing at least 12 sources including at least 3 peer-reviewed articles, 2 business school case studies or HBR articles, and 5 secondary sources (books, interviews, news articles)
Framework application table showing exactly which OB frameworks were used and how they applied to the case
Executive summary (1 page) suitable for sharing with a non-academic audience

criteria: Accurate application of at least five course frameworks (e.g., Big Five, motivation theory, team effectiveness, leadership theories, culture)
Claims are grounded in specific evidence from the case rather than general statements
Integration across levels of analysis (individual, group, organizational) rather than siloed treatments
Recognition of which findings are strongly supported by evidence versus contested or speculative
Clear professional formatting with headings, subheadings, and consistent typography
Framework application table and evidence appendix are clean and navigable
Writing quality is professional throughout with no grammar or spelling errors
Executive summary is self-contained and readable by a non-expert
Leadership lessons are specific, actionable, and traceable to the diagnostic analysis
Alternative explanations for the outcome are considered and addressed rather than ignored
Analysis is honest about what cannot be known from the public record and avoids overclaiming
Conclusions are defensible by a reader who disagrees with them
At least 12 sources cited with proper academic citation format
Sources include required mix of peer-reviewed, case, and secondary materials
Source quality is appropriate (not relying on low-quality blog posts or unverified claims)
Framework application table is complete and accurate
Rigorous multi-framework analysis that integrates individual, group, and organizational levels
Specific evidence-based claims grounded in at least 12 academic and professional sources
Three concrete leadership lessons defensible to a skeptical reader
Professional-quality writing suitable for an MBA-level course

major_assignments.title: Team Effectiveness Intervention Project
major_assignments.description: Work with a real team over three weeks (your project team for this course, a student organization, or a professional team with permission) to diagnose its effectiveness using evidence-based instruments and design a targeted intervention. You will measure psychological safety, team conflict, role clarity, and at least one outcome metric; analyze the results; propose a specific intervention; implement it over at least two weeks; measure again; and report the results honestly, including any failure modes. The point is not to produce a success story; it is to practice the full cycle of diagnosis, intervention, and measurement that distinguishes evidence-based management from consulting theater.
objectives: Use validated instruments (Edmondson's Fearless Organization Scan, Jehn's conflict scale) to measure team states
Design a specific, theory-grounded intervention targeting the weakest area of team effectiveness
Implement the intervention with the team's consent and active participation
Measure outcomes honestly, including null or negative results, and learn from them
deliverables: Team selection and consent document (1 page) identifying the team, its composition, and confirming consent to participate
Baseline diagnosis report (3 to 4 pages) presenting the instrument results, interpretation, and identified priority area
Intervention design document (2 pages) specifying the intervention, the theory supporting it, the implementation timeline, and the success metrics
Follow-up measurement report (3 to 4 pages) presenting post-intervention results, comparison to baseline, and honest analysis of what worked and what did not
Personal learning reflection (1 page) identifying what the student learned about facilitating team change

criteria: Correct application of validated instruments with appropriate administration and scoring
Intervention is grounded in specific OB theory and targets a diagnosable problem
Implementation is well-executed with sustained engagement over at least two weeks
Follow-up measurement uses the same instruments for direct comparison
Baseline and follow-up data are presented in clean charts or tables with labeled axes
Reports are well-formatted and easy to navigate
Intervention design document is specific enough that a colleague could execute it
Writing is professional throughout
