Intervention design reflects sophisticated diagnosis, not a generic team building activity
Results are presented honestly including null or negative findings
Analysis considers alternative explanations for the observed changes (time, other events, demand characteristics)
Learning reflection demonstrates growth in the student's facilitation and measurement skills
All required sections are present with appropriate detail
Consent document is complete and specific
Citations for instruments and theoretical basis are accurate
Final package is a professional deliverable suitable for sharing with the team
Full diagnosis-intervention-measurement cycle executed with a real team
Evidence-based instruments and theoretical grounding throughout
Honest reporting of results including failure modes
Personal learning reflection demonstrating skill development

major_assignments.title: Personal Leadership Development Plan and Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Produce a comprehensive personal leadership development plan integrating self-assessment, 360-degree feedback, course frameworks, and a structured deliberate-practice program. This is the capstone assignment and the most individualized. It requires honest self-assessment, the courage to seek real feedback from real people, and the discipline to translate insight into a specific plan you will actually execute. The plan is due at the end of the semester, and the expectation is that you will implement it during the following year. This is not a hypothetical exercise; it is a commitment you make to yourself and to the people who work with you.
objectives: Integrate multiple assessment inputs (personality, values, 360-degree feedback, situational analysis) into a coherent self-portrait
Identify two to three high-leverage development priorities with specific deliberate-practice goals
Design a feedback and accountability system that will provide honest signal over 12 months
Commit to specific measurable behaviors and explain how success will be evaluated
deliverables: Self-assessment portfolio (4 to 6 pages) integrating Big Five results, values clarification, strengths inventory, and a professional history review that identifies patterns across roles

360-degree feedback summary (2 to 3 pages): actual feedback from at least 4 people (two peers, one supervisor, one direct report or client) collected through structured questions, anonymized and synthesized
Development plan (4 to 5 pages) identifying two to three priority areas, specific behavioral goals, deliberate-practice activities, timelines, and feedback mechanisms
Accountability system (1 page) specifying the people, meetings, metrics, and rituals that will provide ongoing feedback over the next 12 months
Presentation (5 minutes) in the final session sharing one priority area and your commitment to the class

criteria: Self-assessment integrates multiple sources rather than relying on a single instrument
360-degree feedback is from real people with genuine engagement, not rubber-stamped responses
Development goals are specific behaviors that can be practiced and measured
Framework application (deliberate practice, goal-setting, SBI feedback) is accurate and appropriate
Portfolio is professionally formatted with clear sections and navigable structure
Visual elements (charts of strengths, timeline of development) enhance clarity
Presentation slides are clean and professional
Writing quality is appropriate for a document you would share with a senior mentor
Honest and courageous self-assessment rather than curated strengths list
Goals are genuinely challenging and targeted at growth edges, not comfort zones
Feedback mechanism will produce real signal (not just supportive cheerleading)
Accountability system is realistic and specific enough to execute
All required deliverables are complete and properly formatted
360-degree feedback methodology is documented and ethical
Development plan is specific enough that a reader could check in on progress in 6 months
Presentation is clear, honest, and appropriately personal
Integrated self-assessment drawing on multiple sources and real feedback
Specific challenging development priorities with deliberate-practice plans
Honest accountability system designed to produce real signal over 12 months
Commitment presentation demonstrating genuine personal engagement

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes distributed through the semester testing application of OB frameworks to specific leadership and team situations

participation_and_exercises.description: In-class case discussions, simulations, role-play exercises, and team diagnostic workshops; attendance and substantive contribution both count

major_assignments.description: Three major assignments: leadership case analysis, team effectiveness intervention, and personal leadership development plan

midterm_examination.description: In-class exam at week 8 covering personality, motivation, team dynamics, communication, and power with both analytical and applied questions

final_integration_project.description: Final written integration essay connecting course frameworks to a specific management challenge the student faces in their current or next role

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Much of the learning happens through in-class case discussion and role-play; absence cannot be fully made up through readings alone. Students with more than three unexcused absences will have their participation grade reduced.
course_policies.late_submissions: Major assignments submitted late lose 10 percent per day. Quizzes and short exercises cannot be made up unless the absence is arranged in advance or involves a documented emergency. The personal leadership development plan cannot be submitted late because the final-session presentation is a required deliverable.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of readings, cases, and frameworks with classmates is strongly encouraged; learning is social. However, all written submissions and personal reflections must be the student's own work. The leadership case analysis must be individual; the team intervention project involves real team members but the written analysis must be the student's own.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be the student's own and all sources must be properly cited. AI tools may be used for brainstorming and editing but not for drafting substantive analysis; all AI use must be disclosed in an acknowledgments section at the end of each assignment. Fabricating 360-degree feedback responses is a serious integrity violation and will be reported.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need access to business case repositories (Harvard Business School, Ivey), the course textbook, a word processor, and a basic presentation tool. All in-class exercises use only printed materials and whiteboards; devices are not required in class and are discouraged during case discussions to reduce distraction.
